title: MHCFNN: Multihead Attenuated Neuro-Fuzzy based Deep Learning Model for Cybersecurity in Internet of Things
authors: Anand Prakash Dwivedi, Omkar Agrahari
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Cybersecurity is crucial for securing the information or data generated in the Internet of Things paradigm, as it experiences certain challenges, including the problem of data scarcity, data breaches, and increased network traffic. Multiple machine learning and deep learning strategies were designed to overcome such downsides, but their inability to mitigate the computational complexity, overfitting, and poor integrity issues affected their utilization in the attack determination process in the IoT networks. Therefore, in order to address such drawbacks, an effective Multihead attention-enabled Neuro Fuzzy Convolutional Neural Network (MHCFNN) model is proposed in this research. The utilization of the preprocessed and log-transformed data increased the stability and the attack determination accuracy of MHCFNN. Further, the multi-head attention incorporated aided in the accurate determination of the cyber threats by concentrating only on the relevant attack features. Also, the Adam Optimizer included in MHCFNN fine-tuned the model parameters and stipulates the proposed model to attain optimal convergence. The efficacy of the proposed MHCFNN is evaluated using numerous performance metrics using the BoT-IoT dataset, and the proposed research is found to obtain superior performance by attaining 97.09% accuracy, 97.66% sensitivity, and 95.73% specificity for a training percentage of 80.

title: Measurement Techniques for Power Analysis in Side-Channel Attacks on Integrated Circuits
authors: Victor Y. Mikhailov, Alexander S. Tishin, Valery D. Alekseev
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: This article examines the evaluation of hardware security against side-channel attacks (SCA) during the prototyping phase of digital integrated circuits. A study was conducted on the correlation between measurement equipment parameters (bandwidth and sampling rate) and the effectiveness of correlation power analysis (CPA) for an AES-128 encryption algorithm implementation on an FPGA. A specialized measurement methodology is proposed, including calibration, integration of protection mechanisms, leakage differentiation, and effectiveness assessment. Experimental verification focuses on the calibration phase using a specialized measurement setup with variable bandwidth (20–500 MHz) and sampling rate (50–5000 MS/s). The results show that limiting the bandwidth significantly improves the signal-tonoise ratio (SNR) and reduces the number of traces required for key recovery. Furthermore, the study reveals the influence of input data entropy on leakage metrics. These results enable optimization of leakage detection setups, reducing computational and hardware costs while maintaining high assessment reliability.

title: An Ultra-Low Power Built-in-Self-Test Architecture for CMOS Image Sensors Using Adaptive Thresholding and Clock Gating
authors: Krishna Neeraja Periyala, Bungatavala Mithun Chakravarthi, Sri Adibhatla Sridevi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: This paper presents an ultra-low power Built-In-Self-Test (BIST) architecture for CMOS Image Sensors (CIS), incorporating adaptive thresholding and clock gating to improve defect detection efficiency while minimizing power consumption. The proposed architecture identifies pixel defects, including hot and stuck-at faults, by comparing each pixel against a dynamically derived neighborhood-based threshold. The design features a finite state machine (FSM) that governs module activity and enables logic blocks during relevant test phases. Power aware techniques like clock gating and minimal switching are applied to pixel sequencer and defect detection logic to reduce dynamic power by preventing unnecessary switching when modules are idle. Adaptive thresholding enhances robustness by adjusting detection boundaries based in logic pixel statistics. Simulation results confirm <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0 0 \%}$</tex> test coverage for known defects. Compared to always-on implementation, the gate design achieves an estimated 31.3% reduction in dynamic power. This architecture is fully synthesizable and suitable for FPGA or ASIC deployment, making it ideal for low power imaging systems.

title: LEXA-IDS- A Lightweight Explainable Autoencoder-Assisted Intrusion Detection System for Resource-Constrained Iot Networks
authors: R. E Franklin Jino, P. Anantha Christu Raj, Subha Hency Jose
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Intrusion detection in Internet of Things (IoT) environment remains a challenge due to constraints like limited processing capabilities, heterogeneous device behaviors, and fluctuating traffic patterns at network edge. Although existing Deep Learning (DL)-based Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) have achieved high accuracy, increased computational complexity is observed restricting their applicability for realtime deployment. To address this gap, “LEXA-IDS”, a Lightweight Explainable Autoencoder-Assisted IDS model is developed in this paper which is suited for resource-constrained IoT networks. LEXA-IDS comprise of a Micro-Feature Stabilization (MFS) unit that smoothes volatile traffic samples and prunes redundant correlation using low-complexity mechanisms. Further, an Edge-Optimized Sparse Autoencoder (EOS-AE) learns compressed representations of benign behavior and detects anomalies through reconstruction deviations. Finally, an Explainable Feature Attribution Layer (EFAL) produces operator-friendly explanations for identifying dominant features that contribute to each anomaly. For the evaluation process, an open-access IoT dataset for IDS -BotNeTIoT-L01 from Kaggle is used. The approach attained a detection accuracy of 90% and reliable learning capability in both training and testing phase. Preliminary evaluations on the LEXA-IDS model demonstrate reduced computational overhead of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0. 4 m s}$</tex> and improved detection of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 7. 8 \%}$</tex> compared to DNN-IDS, LSTM-RNN, GRU-RNN-IDS, and DRL-IIoT IDS models making it suitable for IoT settings.

title: Design and Implementation of an Autonomous Neural Network Subsystem for Monitoring Computer Systems Digital Components
authors: Alexander K. Krutikov, Dmitry A. Strabykin, Vasily Yu. Meltsov, Aleksei A. Golovin
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: The presented study examines the most common techniques used to automate monitoring and diagnostics of the functioning of computing equipment. The novelty of the approach proposed by the authors lies in the segmentation of the initial diagnostic information collected in real time and the formation of the structure of the neural network subsystem, taking into account the interdependencies of digital components. The sequential connection of neural modules into tiers makes it possible to identify not only potential failures and failures of system elements, but also to predict cascading shutdowns of blocks, nodes, and entire subsystems. The paper describes in detail the key features of the architecture of an intelligent diagnostic and monitoring module operating in automatic mode.

title: Integrative Graph-based Learning for Multi-Omics Characterization of Drug Resistance in Breast Cancer
authors: Malathi K, Jeyabharathi J
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Integration of various omics data is an essential aspect of the research process that enables researchers to use multi-omics data to facilitate cancer diagnosis, subtype classification, predicting drug response, applying precision medicine principles and developing improved ways to manage patients suffering from cancer. In this review, I aim to highlight recent advancements in the use of graph-based machine learning and deep learning approaches for multi-omics integration. Furthermore, this study will assess existing frameworks such as graph neural networks, graph convolutional networks, attention-based models and hybrid deep learning methods to evaluate their ability to model complex biological interactions across heterogeneous datasets through integrated analysis of multi-omics data. Additionally, this research study will examine the use of network-based approaches to prediction of cancers, discovery of biomarkers and designing personalized treatment strategies. Although great strides have been made, there are still many challenges that need to be addressed, such as data heterogeneity, high dimensionality, scalability issues, limited interpretability of models and lack of clinical validation for the new methods. Additionally, advanced techniques that involve multimodal learning and attention mechanisms will require further investigation in order develop effective methods for data fusion. This review is intended to provide a snapshot of key research trends, limitations and future research directions, as well as provide ideas on developing effective computational frameworks for providing data integration of multi-omics data for advancing precision medicine applications.

title: A Unified Multi-Modal System for Image Forgery, Text Plagiarism, and AI-Generated Content Detection
authors: Ambarish A, R Abhiram, Sreelakshmi V S, Anirudh P R, Bharath N, Remya M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The rapid development of content creation technologies and generative AI systems has created more risk of content manipulation, plagiarism and misuse of various types of data. The old systems of plagiarism detection and forgery detection can only operate on one type of content hence it is not applicable to the multimodal threats in the contemporary world. This paper presents Truevision, a multimodal framework designed to detect image forgery, text and PDF plagiarism and AI generated content detection within a single system. The proposed approach integrates deep learning based image analysis for detecting the manipulations like copy move forgery, splicing for image forensics and natural language processing techniques for text similarity and plagiarism detection. The system also incorporates AI generated content detection mechanisms to differentiate human written content from machine generated ones. Experimental evaluations conducted on various datasets indicate that the proposed system enhances detection accuracy compared to the traditional single modal approaches. This unified approach makes it easy to reliably spot complex changes. TrueVision is perfect for real world applications in academia, digital forensics and content verification services.

title: A Study on Extract Bits of Pseudorandom Sequences Generated by Me Operation on the Elliptic Curve
authors: Renta Yamauchi, Natsuo Hayashi, Takeru Miyazaki, Shunsuke Araki, Satoshi Uehara
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: In this paper, we propose an extracting method of pseudorandom bits from sequences generated by Shirase's Me operation on elliptic curves over finite fields, whose security is based on the hardness of the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP). In Shirase's original pseudorandom number generator, only the least significant bit (LSB) of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x$</tex> coordinate of each point generated by the Me operation was extracted. However, since this approach is time-consuming and impractical for real-world use, we conclude a new method that extends the number of extracted bits. We evaluate the statistical properties of the generated sequences from multiple perspectives: 0/1 occurrence rate at each bit position, randomness with respect to initial values, calculation cost, and randomness of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$y$</tex>-coordinates. Furthermore, we compared the ECDLP based pseudorandom number generator with the DLP based method on which it is derived, in order to demonstrate the superiority of the ECDLP based approach. The comparison was conducted from three perspectives: 0/1 occurrence rate at each bit position, randomness with respect to initial values, generation speed and memory usage. Experimental results confirmed that the ECDLP based method achieved higher randomness and stability across all metrics, demonstrating its superiority over the DLP based method. These findings suggest that the generator has strong potential for practical deployment in cryptographic applications requiring secure and efficient pseudorandom number generation.

title: A Hybrid Attribute-Content-Based Access Control Model for Medical Information Systems
authors: Ke Ma, Xinyu Xu, Yizhen Yang, Pingping Xu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Access control is fundamental to facilitating secure data sharing in Medical Information Systems (MIS). While prevalent models like Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC), and Mandatory Access Control (MAC) provide foundational security, their granularity and flexibility are often insufficient for dynamic and collaborative clinical scenarios, such as multi-disciplinary consultations. This limitation creates a critical challenge: balancing stringent security with operational practicality, as overly restrictive policies impede workflows while excessively lenient ones risk sensitive data leakage. To address this, we propose a novel hybrid access control model that enhances the ABAC framework by integrating data content and security level as core attributes for authorization decisions. This composite model enables context-aware and content-driven permissions, effectively supporting secure yet practical data sharing in healthcare environments.

title: Advanced Learning Based Quantum Key Distribution Framework for Practical Secure Communication Networks
authors: ShivaPrasanna. K, B.Rajesh Shyamala Devi, Ravichand S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The integration of Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) with Machine Learning (ML) has developed a high-end, secure model for accurate data transmission and network security. Many existing systems face several issues due to advances in quantum computing. Thus, integrating these models improved the efficiency, accuracy, and feasibility of QKD systems. In this paper, an Advanced Learning Approach (ALA) is combined with the Quantum Key Distribution Framework to refine key generation, error correction, and intrusion detection in quantum communication channels. The proposed ALA also used reinforcement learning algorithms to predict quantum channel fluctuations, dynamically adjust key exchange parameters, and strengthen authentication protocols. Simulation results demonstrate improved key rates, reduced Quantum Bit Error Rates (QBER), and enhanced resilience against eavesdropping attacks. The study contributes to the realization of practical, scalable, and intelligent quantum-secure communication systems—bridging the gap between theoretical quantum security and real-world network deployment.

title: Efficient Signal Detection Method Based on Sparse Bayesian Learning in ZP-OTFS Systems
authors: Xueping Lan, Xiaoxu Zhang, Pingzhi Fan, Zhengquan Zhang, Xianfu Lei, Yi Zhou, Li Hao, Zheng Ma
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Orthogonal time-frequency space (OTFS) modulation has gained significant attention recently for its robust performance in doubly-selective wireless channels. However, designing high-performance signal detection methods for such channels remains challenging. To address this challenge, this paper proposes an efficient detection algorithm, delay-time domain maximum ratio combining with sparse bayesian learning (dtMRC-SBL), focusing on ZP-OTFS modulation. ZP-OTFS systems exhibit quasi-sparse characteristics due to the large number of active users. Consequently, the proposed signal detection operates in two stages. The first stage employs a dtMRC equalizer for coarse detection of high-signal-strength components. Subsequently, undetected low-signal-strength components are filtered. Given the lower signal strength of these components, SBL performs fine detection. The results from both stages are then aggregated to generate the final output. Compared to conventional MRC, the proposed dtMRC algorithm achieves lower complexity and better performance. The integration of the SBL algorithm yields significant performance improvements, demonstrating the effectiveness of the dtMRC-SBL scheme for quasi-sparse signal detection in ZP-OTFS systems.

title: Smart AR-Based Sign Language Recognition System for Real-Time Communication and Translation
authors: Parminder Singh, Kawal Preet Kaur, Samriti
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Sign language users have not yet been properly connected to the rest of the world, through effective communication. In this paper, we present a state-of-the-art real-time sign language recognition and translation system that not only labels continuous hand gestures but also renders them into AR via dynamic speech bubbles. The recognized gestures are mapped into coherent English sentences which are translated into multiple languages (Hindi, Arabic, etc.) and vocalized via TTS synthesis. Our main framework incorporates hand tracking with MediaPipe, a sequential modelling of LSTM-based classification, and Google Translate API for multilingual translation. User interaction is controlled by the Tkinter toolkit within a graphical user interface (GUI). One important feature added to the system is the use of augmented reality (AR). In this setup, AR is used to show the translated or recognized sentence in a small floating bubble next to the user's hand. This appears in real time and makes it easier for users to see and understand the translation. The system uses buffering methods to avoid recognizing gestures too early, which helps the translated sentences flow more smoothly. Tests show that the system recognizes gestures accurately, responds quickly, and works well even with different languages. User feedback also shows that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A R$</tex> makes the experience more responsive and satisfying. Overall, this mix of technologies creates a helpful and easy-to-use tool for communication across different languages, with AR making it more engaging and practical.

title: Threshold Anonymous Credential Scheme Based on Attribute-Based Encryption
authors: Zhengxi Zhong, Hongyang Yan, Weichu Deng, Gongming Zhang, Zhexuan Ni
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Decentralized anonymous credentials (DACs) enable users to prove possession of specific identity attributes without disclosing additional information or relying on a centralized authority. However, existing DAC schemes commonly rely on complex zero-knowledge proofs, resulting in high computational overhead. They also lack sufficient flexibility and efficiency in handling multi-authority environments and supporting complex access policies, while facing limitations in trust assumptions and scalability. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel threshold anonymous credential scheme. Specifically, we introduce a ciphertext-policy attribute-based encryption (CP-ABE) scheme that supports threshold key distribution and aggregation, and leverage it to construct the threshold anonymous credential scheme. Experimental results and security analysis demonstrate that the proposed scheme exhibits high efficiency and flexibility in constructing authentication for complex access policies.

title: Wireless Human Activity Recognition: A Survey of Recent Advances and a Novel Zero-Shot LLM-Based Inference Framework
authors: Zheng Xing, Xuhui Zhang, Wenchao Liu, Huijun Xing, Weibing Zhao, Guanghui Zhang, Shuqiang Wang, Yanyan Shen, Kim-Fung Tsang
abstract: With the rapid advancement of wireless sensing technologies, wireless human activity recognition (HAR) has emerged as a prominent, low-cost, and non-intrusive research area over the past decade. Despite these advances, wireless HAR remains fundamentally challenged by device heterogeneity, human body diversity, and environmental variability, factors that typically require large-scale labeled radio frequency data for model training. This paper first presents a systematic review of representative techniques and publicly available datasets, followed by a comprehensive analysis of recent progress and persistent limitations of HAR. Building on these insights, we propose a novel zero-shot inference framework based on large language models that directly infers human activities from channel state information sequences. By eliminating the need for activity labels from the target dataset, the framework enables faster adaptation to device heterogeneity, human body diversity, and environmental variability. Specifically, we integrate activity model knowledge, which captures the physical interactions between human motion and wireless signal propagation, and global temporal knowledge, which encodes coarse-grained temporal similarity patterns, into structured chain-of-thought prompts. This design empowers the framework to perform semantic and physical reasoning over raw wireless observations. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves robust zero-shot performance, attaining an accuracy of 0.93 and an F1-score of 0.94. This performance significantly outperforms existing zero-shot state-of-the-arts.

title: PunctVR: VR Training for Image-Guided Needle Puncture with a Scaffolded, Self-Directed Framework
authors: Wenqing Liu, Chen Liu, Jiaqi Wang, Yan Zhang, Hangyu Zhou, Zixuan Guo, Aixi Guo, Ziang Qi, Jiannan Ye, Yu Zhang, Qishan Tong, Xubo Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Image-guided percutaneous needle puncture is a critical yet challenging clinical procedure, constrained by the high cognitive demand of mental model construction and manipulation of 3D anatomy via scrolling through 2D cross-sectional images. While virtual reality (VR) simulators provide a risk-free training platform, many focus on simulation fidelity but lack structured, self-directed learning frameworks. In this paper, we present PunctVR, a VR system that incorporates the instructional principles of scaffolding. PunctVR features a training mode employing a phased subgoal workflow and instructional guidance scaffolding, and an assessment-only test mode where both the workflow and enhanced 3D visualization are removed. We conducted a between-subject experiment with 16 physicians, comparing training using a baseline multiplanar reconstruction (MPR) view with a combined MPR + 3D visualization across two difficulty levels. Our test mode results indicate that all trainees significantly improved their performance after training. Furthermore, those who trained with the integrated 3D visualization achieved a greater reduction in puncture time in both easy and hard cases. These findings suggest that PunctVR effectively enhances procedural efficiency in simulated needle puncture training and provides important insights into how learning scaffolding can accelerate skill acquisition and retention for image-guided interventions.

title: Energy-Aware Predictive Offloading in UAV for Containerized Smart Irrigation in IoT Agriculture
authors: Abhishek Pandey, Sourav Kanti Addya
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: This paper introduces a modular and scalable smart irrigation platform that utilizes unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) in concert with a dense network of IoT sensors. The approach centers around predictive scheduling and dynamic decision offloading, realized through lightweight containerized software components orchestrated on Amazon EKS and integrated with AWS IoT Core for secure device management. The evaluation spans a testbed consisting of IoT sensors and UAVs, validating system integration, real-time responsiveness, and cloud-based scalability. Results demonstrate robust communication and effective orchestration, establishing a practical pathway for autonomous, resource-aware irrigation in distributed agricultural environments. Ongoing work aims to expand deployment and refine control intelligence for next-generation sustainable farming.

title: Emphasis Sensitivity in Speech Representations
authors: Shaun Rafael Cassini, Thomas Hain, Anton Ragni
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: This work investigates whether modern speech models are sensitive to prosodic emphasis-whether they encode emphasized and neutral words in systematically different ways. Prior work typically relies on isolated acoustic correlates (e.g., pitch, duration) or label prediction, both of which miss the relational structure of emphasis. This paper proposes a residualbased framework, defining emphasis as the difference between paired neutral and emphasized word representations. Analysis on self-supervised speech models shows that these residuals correlate strongly with duration changes and perform poorly at word identity prediction, indicating a structured, relational encoding of prosodic emphasis. In ASR fine-tuned models, residuals occupy a subspace up to 50% more compact than in pre-trained models, further suggesting that emphasis is encoded as a consistent, lowdimensional transformation that becomes more structured with task-specific learning.

title: What Sensors See, What People Feel: An Exploratory Study of Subjective Collaboration Perception in Mixed Reality
authors: Yasra Chandio, Diana Romero, Salma Elmalaki, Fatima M. Anwar
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: Mixed Reality (MR) enables rich, embodied collaboration; however, it is uncertain whether sensor- and system-logged behavioral signals capture how users experience that collaboration. This disconnect stems from a fundamental gap. Behavioral signals are observable and continuous, while collaboration is interpreted subjectively and shaped by internal states like presence, cognitive availability, and social awareness. Our core insight is that sensor signals serve as observable manifestations of subjective experiences in MR collaboration, and they can be captured through sensor data such as shared gaze, speech, spatial movement, and other system-logged performance metrics. We propose the Sensor-to-Subjective (S2S ) Mapping Framework, a conceptual model that links observable interaction patterns to users’ subjective perceptions of collaboration and internal cognitive states through sensor-based indicators and task performance metrics. To evaluate this model, we conducted an exploratory study with 48 participants across 12 MR groups engaged in a collaborative image-sorting task. Our findings show a correlation between sensed behavior and perceived collaboration, particularly through shared attention and proximity.

title: Scalable Controllable Accented TTS
authors: Henry Li Xinyuan, Zexin Cai, Ashi Garg, Kevin Duh, Leibny Paola García-Perera, Sanjeev Khudanpur, Nicholas Andrews, Matthew Wiesner
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: We propose a method to scale accented TTS training to large, accent-diverse datasets that often lack consistent, high-quality accent labels. Our approach relies on a speech geolocation model to infer accent labels directly from audio. To improve speaker generalization and encourage disentangling speaker from accent we explore timbre augmentation through kNN voice conversion. We validate our approach on CommonVoice by fine-tuning XTTS-v2 with accent labels inferred or improved via geolocation. According to various automated metrics based on embeddings extracted from an accent identification model, the resulting accented TTS model produces speech with better accent fidelity compared to XTTS-v2 fine-tuned on self-reported accent labels in CommonVoice, or other existing accented TTS models. According to human evaluation, it was clear that the geolocation model based data discovery and enhancement improved the naturalness and accent fidelity of generated speech. However, the effect of different data augmentation strategies was less clear.

title: Heat Flow Characteristics of Segmented Laminar Plasma Torch Influenced by Interelectrode Structure Based on Numerical Simulation
authors: Bo Huang, Xiuquan Cao, Yong He, Xin Liu, Chao Li, Fa Zhang
abstract: To further enhance the jet characteristics of the laminar plasma torch, various anode, cathode, and arc chamber configurations have been proposed and investigated through both experimental and theoretical approaches. As a critical component, the interelectrode structure significantly influences not only the jet characteristics but also the operational lifespan of the plasma torch. Therefore, based on a custom-designed segmented laminar plasma torch, this study examines the effect of the interelectrode configuration on the thermal-flow characteristics using a self-developed 2-D simulation model, validated by experimental data. First, typical interelectrode structures employed in the custom torch were analyzed to identify an optimal design. A corresponding simulation model was then developed and verified using the in-house 2-D modeling framework. Subsequently, the influence of the interelectrode on the thermal-flow behavior of the plasma torch was systematically simulated and analyzed, leading to the development of predictive models for jet characteristics. The findings are summarized as follows: The simulated arc voltages agree well with experimental results, showing errors below 3%, which confirms the model’s effectiveness in simulating plasma jet flow behavior. The plasma flow within the torch can be divided into three regions: the arc initiation ring Region A, the interelectrode Region B, and the anode Region C. The interelectrode diameter has minimal influence on temperature and velocity distributions in Region A, whereas both axial temperature and velocity generally decrease with increasing interelectrode diameter in regions B and C. The pilot electrode diameter only affects the temperature and velocity distributions within its corresponding internal section, with a tendency for both to increase as the diameter decreases.

title: Mechanism and Performance Verification of Channel-Hot-Electron Induced Hot-Hole Injection-Based High-Speed Erasure for Split-Gate Charge-Trapping Flash Memory
authors: Zhexuan Li, Xiao Yu, Chunsheng Jiang, Dianyu Qi, Xuan Li, Zhendong Shi, Kun Ren, Dawei Gao, Liyang Pan
abstract: The mechanism of the novel channel-hot-electron induced hot-hole (CHH) injection for high-speed erasure (ERS) in the split-gate charge trapping flash (CTF) cell is systematically investigated. In this scheme, hot holes generated by impact ionization of channel hot electrons exhibit approximately three times higher density and a significantly wider spatial distribution compared to band-to-band tunneling-induced hot-hole (BBHH) ERS. This enhances the hole injection to the charge trapping layer and the trapped hole-electron neutralization, leading to more efficient ERS. In addition, it is experimentally verified that the CHH mechanism enables approximately <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$100\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> faster erasing speed (~0.1 ms) than BBHH, while also achieving better endurance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{{5}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) and retention (ten years at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$175~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C). These advantages make it ideal for high-speed and highly reliable nonvolatile memory (NVM) applications.

title: Direct measurement of transient temperature at the tool–chip interface using a 3D-printed sensor-integrated cutting tool
authors: Chao Wang, Tatsuki Saito, Haruka Yamamoto, Zhitong Xie, Toru Kizaki, Tanibuchi Takahito, Hirosaki Kouji, Kumai Kenji, Keisuke Nagato, Naohiko Sugita
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Due to the extreme thermal-mechanical stress at tool-chip interface, built-in sensors in the cutting tool are prone to failure during machining process despite their high sensitivity. Eroding thermocouple (ETC) can operate at high stress and high wear conditions; however, traditional adhesive bonding methods introduce significant thermal inertia because the low-thermal-con-ductivity bonding materials reduce response speed and cause sys-tematic measurement errors. In this study, we demonstrate for the first time the integration of an ETC at the tool–chip interface using a solid-state diffusion-bonding approach, which creates direct joints between the sensor and substrate materials. A 3D-printed stainless-steel tool was co-sintered with the ETC, achieving seam-less bonding, rapid response, and robust temperature monitoring based on solid-state diffusion. The performance of this sensor-in-tegrated cutting tool was first evaluated through calibration ex-periments and subsequently verified through real cutting tests. To process the measurements, an extended Kalman filter was em-ployed to filter and regulate the data into an equivalent first-order model. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed sensor exhibits superior response speed and robustness compared with the conventional sensor, achieving a 67.8% mean reduction in thermal time constant and improving quasi-steady-state robust-ness by as much as 36.2%. Furthermore, the method enhances measurement accuracy, as evidenced by a distinctly narrowed un-certainty band.

title: An Open-Circuit Fault Diagnosis Method for T-Type Three-Level Inverters Based on Distorted Current Guided by Physical Mechanism
authors: Bowen Wang, Yigang He, Kaixuan Shao, Weiwei Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: T-type three-level inverters are crucial in renewable energy generation for their high efficiency. Nevertheless, open-circuit (OC) fault presents substantial reliability challenges, greatly limiting the deployment and advancement of T-type three-level inverters in the renewable energy sector. In this paper, the current paths of T-type three-level inverter after different OC faults are analyzed in detail. In addition, the fundamental circuit model of the T-type three-level inverter is constructed to reveal the essential differences at extreme points of different OC fault transistors which have the similar fault current distorted characteristics. Building on these foundations, this paper designs an OC fault diagnosis method based on fault distorted current. First, the OC fault detection and fault phase location are achieved through the normalized absolute value of the zero-crossing slope. Subsequently, the OC fault transistors are diagnosed by calculating the average absolute value of the phase current and analyzing the difference of the slope at the extreme points of the fault phase current. The robustness and effectiveness of the method are verified by experimental results. This study integrates theoretical derivation with the fault mechanism of T-type three-level inverters, filling the gap in the theoretical investigation of distorted current from a physical mechanism perspective, and providing guidance for future research and industrial applications in OC fault diagnosis of T-type three-level inverters.

title: Deploying Intelligence in Vehicle Electronics [From the Editor]
authors: Claudio Casetti

title: Audio-CoT: Exploring Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Large Audio Language Model
authors: Ziyang Ma, Zhuo Chen, Yuping Wang, Eng-Siong Chng, Xie Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in tasks involving audio perception and understanding, such as speech recognition and audio captioning. However, their reasoning capabilities-critical for solving complex real-world problems-remain underexplored. In this work, we conduct the first exploration into integrating Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning into LALMs to enhance their reasoning ability across auditory modalities. We evaluate representative CoT methods, analyzing their performance in both information extraction and reasoning tasks across sound, music, and speech domains. Our findings reveal that CoT methods significantly improve performance on easy and medium tasks but encounter challenges with hard tasks, where reasoning chains can confuse the model rather than improve accuracy. Additionally, we identify a positive correlation between reasoning path length and accuracy, demonstrating the potential of scaling inference for advanced instruction-following and reasoning. This study not only highlights the promise of CoT in enhancing LALM reasoning capabilities but also identifies key limitations and provides actionable directions for future research. Index Terms-Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Reasoning, Large Audio Language Model (LALM)

title: Enabling Long FFT Convolutions on Memory-Constrained FPGAs via Chunking
authors: Peter Wang, Neelesh Gupta, Viktor K. Prasanna
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: The need for long-context reasoning has led to alternative neural network architectures besides Transformers and self-attention, a popular model being Hyena, which employs causal 1D-convolutions implemented with FFTs. Long convolutions enable efficient global context mixing, but requirements for intermediate results exceed the 2-3 MB Block RAM capacity of FPGAs. We present a chunked FFT convolution approach enabling 450K length sequence by 450K length filter convolutions on an Alveo U200 FPGA with 2.8 MB BRAM through chunking and overlap-add reconstruction. We find that throughput scales proportionally with chunk size while degrading minimally by 7% for our longest sequences, demonstrating that careful memory management enables deployment of long-context primitives on edge FPGAs without sacrificing performance.

title: Emotional Styles Hide in Deep Speaker Embeddings: Disentangle Deep Speaker Embeddings for Speaker Clustering
authors: Chaohao Lin, Xu Zheng, Kaida Wu, Peihao Xiang, Ou Bai
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Speaker clustering is the task of identifying the unique speakers in a set of audio recordings (each belonging to exactly one speaker) without knowing who and how many speakers are present in the entire data, which is essential for speaker diarization processes. Recently, off-the-shelf deep speaker embedding models have been leveraged to capture speaker characteristics. However, speeches containing emotional expressions pose significant challenges, often affecting the accuracy of speaker embeddings and leading to a decline in speaker clustering performance. To tackle this problem, we propose DTG-VAE, a novel disentanglement method that enhances clustering within a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) framework. This study reveals a direct link between emotional states and the effectiveness of deep speaker embeddings. As demonstrated in our experiments, DTG-VAE extracts more robust speaker embeddings and significantly enhances speaker clustering performance. Our code is available: https://github.com/Toby28/DDSESC.

title: Token-based Attractors and Cross-attention in Spoof Diarization
authors: Kyo-Won Koo, Chan-Yeong Lim, Jee-Weon Jun, Hye-Jin Shim, Ha-Jin Yu
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Spoof diarization identifies “what spoofed when” in a given speech by temporally locating spoofed regions and determining their manipulation techniques. As a first step toward this task, prior work proposed a two-branch model for localization and spoof type clustering, which laid the foundation for spoof diarization. However, its simple structure limits the ability to capture complex spoofing patterns and lacks explicit reference points for distinguishing between bona fide and various spoofing types. To address these limitations, our approach introduces learnable tokens where each token represents acoustic features of bona fide and spoofed speech. These attractors interact with frame-level embeddings to extract discriminative representations, improving separation between genuine and generated speech. Vast experiments on PartialSpoof dataset consistently demon-strate that our approach outperforms existing methods in bona fide detection and spoofing method clustering.

title: Advancing Controllable Music Generation with Latent Rectified Flow Guided by Rhythm and Harmony
authors: Haibin Yu, Jiayi Zhou, Wei Wang, Zhiming Wang, Huijia Zhu, Yanmin Qian
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Rectified flow models have shown considerable potential in various generation tasks, but their capability for music generation remains largely unexplored. These models use ordinary differential equations (ODEs) with linear interpolation, allowing more straightforward distribution transportation compared to diffusion models. In this paper, we present a text-to-music generation framework based on latent rectified flow. Additionally, to further improve its controllability and generation quality, we inject rhythmic and harmonic control signals into the generation process. Extensive objective and subjective evaluations demonstrate that the rectified flow model can generate music of comparable quality as existing systems based on diffusion and language models. Furthermore, integrating external controls enables the rectified flow model to achieve improved performance. Samples are available on https://anonymous.4open.science/w/MusicLRF-demo-2DA3/

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: Canonical Correlation Residual Score-Based Method for Quality-Related Fault Diagnosis in Industrial Processes
authors: Long Gao, Hongquan Ji, Zhiwen Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Fault diagnosis for quality-related variables is crucial for maintaining product quality and operational efficiency of industrial processes. However, modern industrial processes exhibit inherent complexity, making it difficult to accurately diagnose quality-related fault variables. In addition, traditional methods such as contribution plots lack the ability to quantitatively evaluate fault variables. To overcome these deficiencies, this paper proposes a canonical correlation residual score (CCRS)-based fault diagnosis method. Different from conventional methods, the CCRS model is constructed by means of canonical correlation between quality-related and quality-unrelated process variables, where the influence of noises in quality-related variables is reduced. Furthermore, the performance of the CCRS-based diagnostic method is theoretically analyzed. Necessary and sufficient conditions are derived to quantitatively characterize the score of canonical correlation residuals. Finally, an online implementation of CCRS is developed to iteratively update the CCRS model for quality-related fault diagnosis in nonstationary industrial processes. The diagnosis performance of the proposed method has been validated on an experimental three-tank system and the Tennessee Eastman process.

title: Seismic impedance inversion with adaptive group sparse regularization
authors: Qing Xiang, Ronghuo Dai
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Seismic impedance inversion, as a core technology in geophysical exploration, has evolved into a pivotal method for the quantitative prediction of subsurface rock elastic properties within exploration geophysics. Its accuracy and resolution directly impact the precision of hydrocarbon reservoir prediction and characterization. Seismic inversion often faces ill-posedness challenge. Sparse regularization, such as L1-norm, are frequently used to deal with this issue. But the noise interference is still mightiness when dealing with complex geological structures. It is because of inherent feature of sparse regularization which tends to suppress minor geological features. To compensate the limitations of conventional sparse regularization, this paper proposes an another regularization i.e., adaptive group sparse regularization. It is the generalization of the existing group sparse regularization and can adaptively find the optimal results without pre-defined group partition. Based on the theory of group sparse regularization, it can overcome the limitations of sparse regularization. We use adaptive group sparsity to regularize the seismic impedance inversion. To effectively solve the objective function, the variable splitting alternating iteration has been used. Through synthetic data test, it has been verified the feasibility of the proposed method. And then, through actual data tests, it has been verified the advantages in resolution enhancement in complex geological structures compared to the inversion method only with sparse regularization.

title: Self-Anchored Progressive Framework with Noise Mitigation for Unsupervised Camouflaged Object Detection
authors: Shijie Liu, Binwei Xu, Tuo Shen, Guanghui Yue, Qiuping Jiang
abstract: Unsupervised Camouflaged Object Detection (UCOD) presents a significant challenge due to the inherent similarity between camouflaged objects and their backgrounds, compounded by the absence of manual annotations. Although pixel-level pseudo-labeling has proven effective for unsupervised salient object detection (USOD), it is far less reliable for COD, where the concealed and ambiguous nature of camouflaged objects frequently produces noisy pseudo-labels, causing misjudgments, missed detections, and imprecise boundaries. To overcome this, we propose SAPNet, a novel self-anchored progressive framework for UCOD. Rather than depending on noisy pixel-level supervision, we leverage semantically reliable foreground and background regions as high-confidence anchors. This effectively transforms the unsupervised problem into a more robust weakly supervised paradigm, reducing learning difficulty and mitigating overfitting to noise. SAPNet learns camouflaged objects progressively by first emphasizing these confident regions and then exploiting DINO’s contextual awareness to recover complete structures. Central to our framework is the semantic-driven region detector (SDRD), which employs cascaded convolutions and a residual attention projection mechanism to suppress background noise, filter erroneous information, and enhance spatial context, ensuring reliable supervision signals. Furthermore, a region-based context inference module (RCIM) is introduced to iteratively refine object boundaries by integrating multi-level semantic features under the guidance of these refined region-level anchors. Extensive experiments on four benchmark COD datasets demonstrate that SAPNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised methods. The source code of our SAPNet is available at https://github.com/ArloJie/SAPNet.

title: Beyond Diagonal RIS: A New Frontier for 6G Internet of Things Networks
authors: Wali Ullah Khan, Chandan Kumar Sheemar, Eva Lagunas, Symeon Chatzinotas
abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) technology is emerging as a key enabler for upcoming sixth-generation (6G) and beyond wireless networks, shifting from passive radio environments to programmable wave propagation. While diagonal RIS (D-RIS) offers benefits, its restricted manipulation capability limits overall performance. This paper explores the novel concept of beyond-diagonal RIS (BD-RIS) for the Internet of Things (IoT), which introduces non-diagonal elements in the scattering matrix to achieve finer control of electromagnetic wavefronts. We first outline the drawbacks of conventional D-RIS and present representative BD-RIS architectures with their operating modes. We then emphasize the distinctive advantages of BD-RIS in supporting advanced 6G IoT applications. A case study on BD-RIS-enabled vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) communication demonstrates potential performance improvements. Finally, we identify key challenges that remain and propose new research directions to advance energy-efficient, scalable, and reliable 6G IoT networks.

title: Reliability of Lexical Richness Measures for ASR-Based Children’s Speech Assessment
authors: Imen Talbi, Christopher Gebauer, Lars Rumberg, Edith Beaulac, Hanna Ehlert, Jörn Ostermann
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Early language acquisition is vital for child cognitive and social development. Timely identification of language delays enables effective interventions. Traditional Language Sample Analysis (LSA) methods, the gold standard for assessing language acquisition, are time-consuming, making automation a promising alternative. Yet, analyzing spontaneous speech for linguistic features is hard due to errors from spoken language processing (SLP) systems. This study uses the KidsTALC dataset to explore the link between SLP performance and LSA measures of early language acquisition. We use a child-adapted ASR model to assess LSA measures, focusing on lexical diversity, on both human and machine transcripts. Our findings show that some LSA measures, like HD-D and vocd-D, are robust to imperfect ASR, with correlations over 0.9. This suggests that ASR-driven LSA, with proper methods and metric selection, can offer valuable insights for early language evaluation and aid in therapy decisions.

title: Analysing the Language of Neural Audio Codecs
authors: Joonyong Park, Shinnosuke Takamichi, David M. Chan, Shunsuke Kando, Yuki Saito, Hiroshi Saruwatari
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: This study presents a comparative analysis of the statistical and linguistic properties of neural audio codecs (NACs). We investigate discrete speech tokens produced by various NAC models, examining their adherence to linguistic statistical laws such as Zipf’s law and Heaps’ law, as well as their entropy and redundancy. To assess how these token-level properties relate to semantic and acoustic preservation in synthesized speech, we evaluate intelligibility using error rates of automatic speech recognition, and quality using the UTMOS score. Our results reveal that NAC tokens, particularly 3-grams, exhibit language-like statistical patterns. Moreover, these properties, together with measures of information content, are found to correlate with improved performances in speech recognition and resynthesis tasks. These findings offer insights into the structure of NAC token sequences and inform the design of more effective generative speech models.

title: IEEE WiCAS and YPCAS Host Joint Event IEEE at ISICAS 2015 in Qingdao to Explore Career Reshaping in the AI-Powered Era [CASS Conference Highlights]
authors: Yanan Sun, Xiaozhe Wang
abstract: The joint event hosted by IEEE Women in Circuits and Systems (WiCAS) and the Young Professionals of the Circuits and Systems Society (YPCAS) on 26 October 2025, in Qingdao brought together leading academics, industry experts, and emerging professionals to explore how artificial intelligence is reshaping careers in the circuits and systems field. Under the theme “Beyond AI: Reshaping Your Career in the AI-Powered Era,” the program featured keynote presentations covering advancements in integrated circuit reliability, biomedical microsystems, and AI-driven hardware design. The event concluded with an interdisciplinary roundtable discussion focused on the evolving role of AI in academia and industry, the critical competencies needed to remain competitive, and the high-value skills that complement AI technologies. This gathering fostered meaningful dialogue and provided strategic insights to help professionals navigate an increasingly AI-centric technological landscape.

title: Cross-Ambiguity Function Hierarchical Optimization Doppler Estimation for Underwater Acoustic OFDM Communication
authors: Bin Li, Zhuoya Huang, Xiujing Gao, Fang Wang
abstract: Underwater sensor networks (USNs) are essential for marine monitoring, yet their performance is constrained by the instability of underwater acoustic (UWA) communication links. Orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) is widely adopted in UWA systems for its high data rate capability and support for multi-user access, however, it remains highly sensitive to Doppler effects, which degrades demodulation performance.Hence, Doppler estimation and compensation are essential in UWA OFDM communications. The Doppler scale is typically estimated by the calculation of the optimal cross-ambiguity function (CAF) between the locally generated and received signals. However, this type of algorithm generates prohibitive computational complexity due to the necessity of exhaustive search over a two-dimensional delay and Doppler factor space. In this paper, a cross-ambiguity function hierarchical optimization (CAF-HO) framework is proposed to partition the Doppler estimation into two stages for low complexity Doppler estimation. Furthermore, the particle swarm optimization (PSO) is integrated with adaptive parameter control to effectively navigate the Doppler estimation landscape. The numerical simulation and sea trial experiment demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed strategy over the CAF estimation and block estimation approaches. By diminishing computational burden and energy consumption, this framework facilitates the large-scale deployment of USNs crucial for marine monitoring, climate research, and maritime security.

title: Equilibrium Contrastive Learning for Imbalanced Image Classification
authors: Sumin Roh, Harim Kim, Ho Yun Lee, Il Yong Chun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: The data imbalance issue is critical in image classification. Contrastive learning (CL) is a predominant technique in image classification, but they showed limited classification performance when the class distribution in a training dataset is skewed (i.e., an imbalanced dataset). Recently, several supervised CL methods have been proposed particularly to promote an ideal regular simplex geometric configuration in the representation space—characterized by intra-class feature collapse and uniform inter-class mean spacing—especially for imbalanced datasets. In particular, existing prototype-based methods include class representatives, i.e., prototypes, as additional samples to achieve a more balanced treatment of all classes. However, the existing supervised CL methods for imbalanced datasets still suffer from two major limitations. First, they do not consider the alignment between the class means/prototypes and linear classifiers, which could lead to poor generalization. Second, existing prototype-based methods treat class prototypes as only one additional sample per class, making their influence depend on the number of class instances in a batch and causing unbalanced contributions across classes. To address these limitations, we propose Equilibrium Contrastive Learning (ECL), a supervised CL framework designed to promote geometric equilibrium in the representation space, where class features, means, and classifiers are harmoniously balanced under data imbalance. The proposed ECL framework uses two main strategies. First, ECL promotes the representation geometric equilibrium (i.e., a regular simplex geometry characterized by collapsed class samples and uniformly distributed class means), while balancing the contributions of class-average features and class prototypes. Second, ECL establishes a classifier-class center geometric equilibrium by aligning classifier weights and class prototypes in the representation space. We ran experiments with three long-tailed natural image datasets, the CIFAR-10-LT, CIFAR-100-LT and ImageNet-LT benchmark datasets, and the two imbalanced medical image classification datasets, the ISIC 2019 benchmark and our constructed lung cancer chest computed tomography dataset. Results with the five imbalanced datasets show that ECL outperforms existing state-of-the-art supervised CL methods designed for imbalanced/long-tailed classification. Codes are available at this link.

title: IEEE Systems, Man, and Cybernetics Society Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems

title: Towards AGI-Enabled Solutions For IoX Layers Bottlenecks In Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking Space
authors: Amar Khelloufi, Huansheng Ning, Sahraoui Dhelim, Jianguo Ding
abstract: The integration of the Internet of Everything (IoX) and emerging Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) has given rise to a transformative paradigm aimed at addressing critical bottlenecks across the sensing, network, and application layers in Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking (CPST) ecosystems. In this survey, we provide a systematic and comprehensive review of pre-AGI and AGI-inspired approaches for IoX, focusing on three key components: sensing-layer data management, network-layer protocol optimization, and application-layer decision-making frameworks. Specifically, this survey explores how pre-AGI and AGI-inspired strategies can mitigate IoX bottlenecks by leveraging adaptive sensor fusion, edge preprocessing, and selective attention mechanisms at the sensing layer. At the network layer, the survey examines solutions to challenges such as protocol heterogeneity and dynamic spectrum management, including approaches based on neuro-symbolic reasoning, active inference, and causal reasoning. Furthermore, the survey investigates AGI-inspired frameworks for managing identity and relationship explosion at the application layer. Key findings suggest that emerging AGI-inspired approaches offer novel solutions to sensing-layer data overload, network-layer protocol heterogeneity, and application-layer identity explosion. These solutions include adaptive sensor fusion, edge preprocessing, and semantic modeling. The survey underscores the importance of cross-layer integration, quantum-enabled communication, and ethical governance frameworks for future AGI-driven IoX systems. Finally, the survey identifies unresolved challenges, including computational requirements, scalability, and real-world validation, and calls for further research to fully realize AGI’s potential in addressing IoX bottlenecks. We believe that AGI-enhanced IoX is emerging as a critical research field at the intersection of interconnected systems and advanced AI.

title: Wildfire Spread Forecasting with Deep Learning
authors: Nikolaos Anastasiou, Spyros Kondylatos, Ioannis Papoutsis
abstract: Accurate prediction of wildfire spread is crucial for effective risk management, emergency response, and strategic resource allocation. In this study, we present a Deep Learning (DL)-based framework for forecasting the final extent of burned areas, using data available at the time of ignition. We leverage a spatio-temporal dataset that covers the Mediterranean region from 2006 to 2022, incorporating remote sensing data, reanalysis meteorological observations, vegetation maps, land cover classifications, topography data, and thermal anomalies. To evaluate the influence of temporal context, we conduct an ablation study examining how the inclusion of pre- and post-ignition data affects model performance, benchmarking the temporal-aware DL models against a baseline trained exclusively on ignition-day inputs. Our results indicate that multi-day observational data substantially improve predictive accuracy. Particularly, the best-performing model, incorporating a temporal window of four days before to five days after ignition, improves both the F1 score and the Intersection over Union by almost 5% in comparison to the baseline on the test dataset. We publicly release our dataset and models to enhance research into data-driven approaches for wildfire modeling and response.

title: QuadQ: Quadratic-Based Value Decomposition for Cooperative Policy Optimization in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
authors: Siying Wang, Ruoning Zhang, Yang Zhou, Jinliang Shao, Yuhua Cheng
abstract: Dear Editor, This letter introduces a value decomposition method based on a quadratic function for multi-agent reinforcement learning, aiming to derive cooperative policies among agents modeled by artificial neural networks. By constructing a quadratic function with specific parameter constraints, it ensures that the function achieves a global maximum when the local optimal actions are used as inputs. Based on this structured quadratic function, a novel value decomposition algorithm is proposed to facilitate optimal cooperative policy training within the centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) framework. Furthermore, a theoretical analysis is provided to guarantee the satisfaction of the individual-global max (IGM) condition. Finally, simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method in solving relative overgeneralization problems.

title: XS-FedPRS: Explainable and Secure Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving Genomic Analytics in Federated Healthcare Networks
authors: Josiah Ayoola Isong, Simeon Okechukwu Ajakwe, Dong-Seong Kim
abstract: In federated healthcare networks (FHNs), collaborative learning enables distributed genomic intelligence across hospital sites and clinical institutions without centralizing sensitive patient data. However, federated polygenic risk prediction (PRS) across heterogeneous healthcare networks faces three critical challenges: population-stratified rare variant distributions that diverge across clinical sites, byzantine attacks mimicking rare variant signals, and lack of interpretability for clinical decision support. We present XS-FedPRS, a framework that decouples common variants (global aggregation) from rare variants (population-specific clustering), defends against byzantine attacks through Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium testing and gradient plausibility analysis ,and provides multi-level explainability for clinical decision support. Proof-of-Authority-and-Association (PoA2) ensures auditable governance across participating sites. Evaluation across three genomic cohorts of increasing scale and realism—CINECA synthetic (483 samples), 1000 Genomes Phase 3 (2,504 samples, 5 continental populations), and msprime-simulated (50,000 samples, Out-of-Africa demography)—demonstrates that XS-FedPRS achieves 84.4% AUC on the largest cohort under population-stratified non-IID partitioning, detects gradient poisoning, model poisoning, sybil, and combined byzantine attacks with perfect precision and recall (F1=1.0), maintains privacy (0.026 MIA advantage), and incurs only 3.3% communication overhead versus FedAvg. XS-FedPRS enables trustworthy, interpretable federated genomic analytics across heterogeneous healthcare networks devices for precision medicine.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: AoI-Aware Privacy-Preserving Task Allocation in Vehicular Crowdsensing
authors: Jialing Hong, Yantao Yu, Jingxiao Ma, Cheng Huang, Rongxing Lu
abstract: In Vehicular Crowdsensing (VCS) systems, task allocation is a critical process that connects sensing demands with distributed vehicular resources. Its effectiveness fundamentally relies on optimizing the spatio-temporal features of vehicles, where information freshness is quantified by the Age of Information (AoI). However, task allocation that aims to jointly optimize spatial coverage and AoI requires the collection of precise trajectory and region data, which raises serious privacy concerns and becomes a key barrier to practical deployment. To achieve privacy-preserving and spatio-temporally optimized task allocation, this paper proposes a novel AoI-aware privacy-preserving task allocation framework based on secret sharing. The framework encodes both task regions and vehicle trajectories into timestamp-associated arrays and splits them into lightweight random shares. Through collaborative computation between two fog servers and a cloud platform, it enables secure evaluation of vehicle-task spatio-temporal matching without exposing the original data. Furthermore, we design a budget-constrained, spatio-temporally near-optimal vehicle selection algorithm that simultaneously maximizes spatial coverage and minimizes AoI under budget constraints. Security analysis and experimental results demonstrate that our scheme effectively protects location privacy while achieving near-optimal spatio-temporal coverage. Moreover, it significantly reduces both communication and computational overhead compared with a homomorphic encryption baseline, confirming its practical effectiveness for time-sensitive VCS applications.

title: Joint Overt–Covert Dual-Robust Beamforming for Satellite Communications
authors: Ce Guo, Huaiqi Jia, Ying Wang, Yi Huang
abstract: This paper investigates a dual-robust beamforming design for covert satellite communication, where covert transmission is embedded within an overt channel. While prior studies on covert communication over overt channels typically address robustness for covert user under wiretap channel (WC) uncertainty, achieving simultaneous robustness for both overt and covert users remains challenging in satellite-to-ground links with uncertain channel state information (CSI). We propose a dual-robust beamforming framework that jointly designs overt and covert beamformers to maximize the minimum covert rate, subject to the overt user’s rate outage constraints (ROCs) and covertness constraints. The resulting optimization problem is intrinsically nonconvex due to CSI and WC uncertainties, variable coupling, and the absence of closed-form expressions for the ROCs. To solve the problem, we reformulate the problem into a sequence of convex subproblems by utilizing semidefinite relaxation, the S-procedure, large deviation inequality, and fractional programming. We then develop a dual-robust beamforming algorithm (DRBA) that yields near-optimal beamforming vectors. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves superior covert communication performance while ensuring robust service for both overt and covert users, outperforming benchmark schemes.

title: Rotating Scaled Synchronization of Networked Agent Systems With Hybrid Time Delays
authors: Weihao Li, Mengji Shi, Lei Shi, Boxian Lin, Kaiyu Qin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: Rotational coordination is a canonical collective behavior in natural and engineered systems, including satellite constellations and swarm robotics. However, hybrid time delays, arising from sensing/measurement, computation, and communication, can degrade coordination and compromise stability. With this in mind, this paper addresses the rotating scaled synchronization problem for second-order networked agent systems (NASs) subject to hybrid time delays. A fully distributed control scheme is proposed that drives agents to rotate on concentric circles with a prescribed scaling vector. Unlike conventional approaches that enforce identical trajectories, the proposed design admits heterogeneous circular orbits while synchronizing angular velocity and phase relationships. Theoretically, we establish sufficient conditions for rotating scaled synchronization and derive an explicit maximum admissible delay bound (delay margin) via a frequency-domain characterization of the closed-loop dynamics, providing a tractable certificate of stability and synchronization under hybrid time delays. Finally, simulation results are presented to validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed approach.

title: The Franklin Stove: An Unintended American Revolution—Joyce E. Chaplin (New York, NY, USA: Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2025, 422 pp.)
authors: Karl D. Stephan
abstract: Presents reviews for the following list of books, (The Franklin Stove: An Unintended American Revolution—Joyce E. Chaplin (New York, NY, USA: Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2025, 422 pp.)).

title: Video-Based Cross-Domain Emotion Recognition Via Sample-Graph Relations Self-Distillation
authors: Dongliang Chen, Guihua Wen, Pengcheng Wen, Huihui Li, Pei Yang, Chuyun Chen, Bao Wang
abstract: Facial expression recognition (FER) under cross-domain scenarios is a challenging task due to the datasets bias and distribution variation. However, existing cross-domain FER models are limited to the static images, less attention has been given to dynamic facial videos. The presence of domain discrepancies at both the spatial frames and temporal sequence levels further complicates the emotion recognition. To this end, this paper proposes a novel unsupervised video-based cross-domain FER method called Sample-graph Relations Self-distillation (SRS). Specifically, SRS first constructs a dual sample-graph transformer (DSGT) by integrating a frame-based optimal transport graph (FOTG) and a video-based hyperbolic-geometry graph (VHG) to capture critical inter- and intra-domain sample relations from both frame-level and video-level perspectives, which facilitates the interaction of similar emotion videos under two domains. Then, a domain graphs alignment is designed to align the intra-domain graphs in both frame and video views, respectively. This enables the consistency of topological structures of two domains to enhance similar emotion videos capturing across domains. Furthermore, to effectively improve the domain generalization ability of base network, SRS adapts a self-distillation manner based on selective source and target emotion soft labels to transfer the reliably sample-graph relations from DSGT to base network. Experimental results on cross-domain settings based on several video-based FER databases show that SRS outperforms the state-of-the-art methods.

title: Demonstration of Improvement in On-Resistance versus Breakdown Voltage Trade-Off for Top-Gate High-Voltage InGaO TFTs
authors: Jiayao Hao, Huibo Liu, Peiyan Hong, Dongying Qiu, Zhenyu Zhang, Xuefei Li
abstract: In this work, we report a method to improve the trade-off between the breakdown voltage (BV) and specific on-resistance (R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON,sp</sub>) of top-gate amorphous indium gallium oxide (IGO) high-voltage thin-film transistors (HV TFTs) with selective F plasma treatment for offset regions. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed that F plasma treatment effectively increased the oxygen vacancy (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">O</sub>) concentration in the IGO film, improving the conductivity of the offset regions. Accordingly, the offset-region resistance of top-gate HV IGO TFTs is reduced by 58.3%, while its pristine threshold voltage (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TH</sub>) of -2.2 V and subthreshold swing (SS) of 81 mV/dec are preserved. Specifically, the top-gate HV IGO TFT with L<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">GD</sub> = 10 μm achieves a high BV of 1015 V and an R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON,sp</sub> of 79 mΩ·cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Our results show the great potential of top-gate HV IGO TFTs in high-voltage and high-power applications.
