title: SP $^{2}$ Join: Secure and Practical Multi-Table Pairwise Equi-Join Queries over Encrypted Databases
authors: Sen Zhao, Siyi Lv, Zheli Liu, Fuchun Guo, Xiaofeng Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Practical equi-join queries over encrypted databases aim to provide clients with flexible query capabilities while safeguarding data confidentiality. So far, the most promising and practical solution is CHT, which is based on Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs). However, such schemes can only support two-table equi-join queries, which are rather limited compared to Multi-Table Pairwise Equi-Join (MTPEJ). Furthermore, in terms of efficiency, throughput, and access pattern, the join implemented in TEEs is still problematic compared to a practical and secure scheme. In this work, we focus on this important type of equi-join, which facilitates join across any two tables among multiple tables. Specifically, we propose SP <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> Join in this paper, the first MTPEJ framework that supports secure pairwise equi-join among multi-table by leveraging a novel oblivious pipeline join algorithm in TEEs. To validate our approach, we evaluate SP <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> Join in MySQL using the benchmarks and real datasets, and the results demonstrate that SP <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> Join is superior to the state-of-the-art in terms of secure equi-join. Particularly, our pipeline join query time is only 2.68% of CHT, yet throughput can reach up to 16.56 × of CHT. Even for MTPEJ, it is still 14.95% of CHT when implementing pairwise equi-join among 10 tables, with a time cost of approximately 140.30 ms.

title: Security-Trust-Determinism Co-Design Using Hybrid Intrusion Detection With Temporal Modeling for Real-Time Publish-Subscribe Middleware
authors: Ramakrishna Penaganti
abstract: This paper proposes a trust-aware hybrid intrusion detection (IDS) for real-time pub/sub middleware that jointly targets security effectiveness and determinism preservation. The proposed approach fuses a low-latency inline detector with a temporal convolutional network (TCN) through a calibrated score-level combination, and then aggregates the fused evidence using a lightweight Bayesian trust engine to maintain continuous trust scores for middleware entities. Trust-guided enforcement is constrained by deterministic quality of service (QoS) budgets to avoid degrading real-time guarantees. Experiments on the publicly available UNSW-NB15 benchmark demonstrate that the hybrid design improves separability and robustness to time-dependent attack behaviors while remaining compatible with real-time execution. The obtained results of the proposed work establish a practical security–trust–determinism trade-off for deploying hybrid machine learning-based IDS in real-time middleware environments.

title: Load Disaggregation in Residential Appliances with Multiple States: Comparative Techniques
authors: Carlos A. Urzagasti, Isaac S. De Freitas, Oswaldo H. Ando, Euzeli C. Dos Santos, Fabiano Salvadori
abstract: This study presents a comparative analysis of load disaggregation techniques applied to residential appliances with multiple operational states, aiming to enhance Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring (NILM) capabilities. The research evaluates three appliances with multi-state consumption patterns (refrigerator, washing machine, air conditioner) using DATAPORT and UK-DALE datasets across four experimental scenarios: intra-household, inter-household, multi-household, and multi-dataset. The methodology employs the Torch-NILM framework to implement and compare five deep learning architectures: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Sequence-to-Point (Seq2Point), Denoising Autoencoder (DAE), and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). Results reveal distinct technique-appliance specializations in the intra-household scenario: Seq2Point achieves optimal refrigerator disaggregation (F1-score: 0.92, MAE: 18.0W, RMSE: 32.0W), GRU excels for washing machines (F1-score: 0.76, MAE: 22.0W, RMSE: 90.0W), and CNN demonstrates superior air conditioner performance (F1-score: 0.85, MAE: 24.0W, RMSE: 80.0W). Cross-household transfer induces 15-20% performance degradation, with GRU exhibiting superior robustness (refrigerator: 0.78, washing machine: 0.68) compared to CNN and Seq2Point. Multi-household training recovers 8-10% of lost performance through implicit regularization from diverse usage patterns. multi-dataset evaluation (DATAPORT to UK-DALE) reveals catastrophic degradation (40-50% loss), with maximum F1-scores of 0.55 (GRU, refrigerator) attributed to sampling rate mismatch (1s versus 6s), regional electrical differences (120V versus 230V, 60Hz versus 50Hz), and cultural usage pattern divergence. These findings quantify fundamental limitations in current NILM architectures for cross-domain generalization and establish empirical benchmarks for technique selection based on appliance characteristics and deployment constraints.

title: FedAligns: A Federated Learning-Based IoT Intrusion Detection Method with Direction-Aligned Updates
authors: Keyuan Qiu, Zhejie Xu, Jianlin Lu, Xiaohui Xu, Shuangshi Zhao, Meifang Yan, Tao Luo, Dejie Yang, Zhigang Li
abstract: IoT intrusion detection faces critical challenges from device heterogeneity, non-iid data distributions, and malicious updates. To address these, we propose FedAligns, a robust and efficient federated learning intrusion detection framework. First, we design a Temporal Attention Autoencoder that integrates TCN, GRU, and self-attention to capture local patterns and global dependencies in complex IoT traffic, enhancing feature representation. Second, we introduce a dual-aggregation algorithm, AlignMSE, to tackle non-iid heterogeneity and malicious threats. Its first phase filters anomalous updates via temporal direction alignment and sign consistency to ensure robust aggregation. Its second phase dynamically allocates weights based on local Mean Squared Error, prioritizing high-performing clients to better adapt to non-iid data. Experiments on N-BaIoT and ToN IoT datasets show FedAligns significantly outperforms traditional methods in accuracy and robustness, demonstrating superior resilience against malicious updates in non-iid scenarios.

title: From Structure to Semantics: Hypergraph-Based AR Assembly Guidance with LLM-Mediated Narration
authors: Xinda Liu, Bowei Zhang, Jiaju Xu, Jian Wu, Guohua Geng, Lili Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Effective Augmented Reality (AR) guidance for complex assembly faces a dual challenge: the inability of conventional liaison graphs to represent procedural logic, and the cognitive burden imposed by visual instructions. We argue that the solution requires a more expressive structure to overcome these representational deficits and a narration approach to mediate instruction complexity. Our method first employs an assembly hypergraph to capture the task's hierarchical information, from which an A* search algorithm generates an optimal assembly path. Then a Large Language Model (LLM)-mediated narration workflow is designed to address the ergonomic deficiencies of the machine-centric path. It employs an optimizer to improve fluency, followed by a narrator that crafts the steps into an intuitive instruction narration. A within-subjects user study (N = 24) revealed a progressive enhancement from our method's components. The transition from a liaison-graph baseline to the hypergraph alone improved objective outcomes by reducing task time and errors and improving subjective ratings (SUS, NASA-TLX, TAM, and ARI). Subsequently, augmenting the LLM-mediated narration maintained these gains while lowering cognitive load and elevating user experience and usability. Our findings indicate the value of our AR assembly design and discuss the opportunities of using LLM as a mediation layer for better user interaction.

title: Challenges of Precueing Instructions for Compound Task Procedures in Mixed Reality
authors: Ahmed Rageeb Ahsan, Andrew W. Tompkins, Eric Ragan, Jaime Ruiz, Ryan P. McMahan
publish: Proceedings of the 51st Graphics Interface Conference 2025
abstract: None

title: Accuracy-Efficiency Trade-Off Analysis of Deep Learning Models for Breast Cancer Diagnosis Using Ultrasound Images
authors: Sunny Kumar, Abhijeet Dhanotiya, Jasmine Selvakumari Jeya I, Tushan Kumar Sinha, Anuj Gupta, Aldrin Joan Pandian W
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative analysis of four deep learning architectures on an ultrasound image dataset of 2104 training and 529 test images of human breast, differentiating between three classes (benign, malignant, or normal). These models are evaluated on multiple parameters -accuracy loss, Cohen's Kappa coefficient, Area Under Curve (AUC), and computational efficiency. The results show InceptionV3 achieves the highest overall accuracy of 94.14 % with a Cohen's Kappa score of 0.9121, followed by DenseNet121, which provided competitive performance with 93.01 % accuracy and the lowest test loss of 0.2024. VGG16 delivered an accuracy of 92.06 %, whereas MobileNetV2 showed a lower accuracy of 86.96 %. Robust comparisons between models are ensured using statistical significance testing and highly sophisticated evaluation metrics. By tackling issues with model selection, accuracy over computational efficiency trade-offs, and deployment considerations, this study offers helpful insights for well-informed choices in deep learning applications.

title: Context-Aware Explanation Drift Detection (CA-EDD): For Plant Disease Severity Estimation
authors: Sonu Varghese K, R Satheesh Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Deep learning models have shown high accuracy in automated plant disease classification; however, their black-box nature limits adoption in precision agriculture, where biological validity and interpretability are critical. Conventional Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) methods, such as Grad-CAM, generate visual saliency maps that often lack alignment with true pathological symptoms, leading to predictions that are accurate yet biologically inconsistent. This paper proposes SymptomConsistency Guided Explainable AI (SCG-XAI), a novel ContextAware Explanation Drift Detection (CA-EDD) framework that validates model reasoning against established plant pathology principles. The framework integrates an explanation generator, an explanation embedding module that encodes attribution maps into structured symptom descriptors capturing lesion color, texture, and spatial distribution, a temporal drift analyzer to detect shifts in model reasoning across disease severity stages, and a context integration layer that constrains explanation validity using agronomic criteria, specifically the Relative Lesion Height (RLH) and the Standard Evaluation System (SES) for rice sheath blight caused by Rhizoctonia solani. By evaluating the semantic consistency between model explanations and physiological disease symptoms, SCG-XAI enables the detection of logic drift that is not reflected in conventional performance measures. Experimental results on a multi-severity rice sheath blight dataset demonstrate that SCG-XAI maintains competitive classification accuracy while ensuring that model explanations are biologically consistent and trustworthy for real-world field deployment.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Recruitment: Frameworks and Innovations
authors: Pankaj Khurana, Jimmy Singla
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Now a days, the entire recruitment process is changed with the automation and artificial intelligence. There is no doubt that candidate screening, evaluation and shortlisting processes have been changed with the help of intelligent systems. In this paper, the latest developments of artificial intelligence - based frameworks and innovations for recruitment processes are reviewed. The existing studies use machine learning, natural language processing and deep learning techniques to improve resume parsing, job matching and interview analysis. Some existing studies are based on the combination of blockchain technology with gamification. In these studies, hiring process is more transparent, fair and engaging for all the stakeholders. The emerging trends from the existing studies are hybrid transformer-based models and smart recommendation systems. These models and systems are able to make sensible recommendations for employers of the organizations. Further there are some issues with these artificial intelligence-based systems for recruitment. These issues are data bias, privacy and explainability. Basically, this paper offers understanding of how intelligent systems are transforming the process of recruitment through automation and incorporation of artificial intelligence.

title: Detection of Algorithmic Bias in AI-Based Recruitment Systems Using Explainable AI
authors: P. Hari Krishna, K. Uday Kumar Reddy, G. Leela Aravinda, V. Anjali, C. Bhavana Reddy, SK. Abdur Rehman
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: This project is about an intelligent recruitment forecasting system that uses advanced machine learning models to evaluate and score candidates for hiring. The system combines Logistic Regression, Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), and XGBoost to make dependable predictions from candidate facts like age, experience, and education level, the skills, certifications, and requirements of the job. In order to raise the quality of data, the system gets job and candidate details ready, calculates skill intersections, and changes the categorical features into suitable numerical formats. The problem with the class that was solved was by using Random Over Sampling, so that the models remain stable and not biased during the training. The performance of the models is very well measured through the use of the different metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score together with the visualization tools such as confusion matrices and ROC curves, thereby enabling the recruiters to benchmark the algorithms and pick the one that is most effective for candidate evaluation. In order to open up more, the system uses Explainable AI methods like SHAP (Shapley Additive Explanations) and LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations). SHAP offers both global and local feature importance, while LIME clarifies how individual candidate features influence a prediction. These explanations make it possible for the recruiters to know the “hired” or “rejected” decisions' logic and thus help in establishing fairness, trust, and accountability. To sum up, this project is a proof of concept that the use of machine learning and explainable AI in recruitment can lead to a more secure decision-making process, fewer biases from algorithms, and a more consistent and morally right way of choosing candidates.

title: Towards Trustworthy LLMs: A Hybrid RAG-Enhanced, Attention-Driven Multi-Metric Framework for Model Evaluation and Comparison
authors: Suryaram S, Getzi Jeba Leelipushpam Paulraj, Stewart Kirubakaran S, Immanuel Johnraja Jebadurai
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The rapid development of Large Language Models has transformed the field of NLP, enabling a range of applications from auto-generated content to intelligent conversational systems. This work proposes an overall hybrid interpretability and evaluation framework for transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs), including multi-model comparison, Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), attention-based mechanistic analysis, and multimetric performance scoring. It processes user queries with optional contextual information extracted from PDF, text, or CSV files and enhances the input using an offline RAG mechanism built with SentenceTransformer embeddings and FAISS indexing. Clickable evidence links retrieved from a locally cached NewsAPI dataset provide additional external context to ground the responses and reduce hallucination, improving their factual reliability. The interpretability module computes token-level attention importance directly from the encoder, producing normalized attention maps and visual heatmaps. Output length and inference speed are also measured, and all four metrics contribute to a weighted overall score: 40% faithfulness, 30% length, 15% coherence, and 15% speed. A comparison engine runs each model in sequence, using strict memory management, attention fallback handling, and CPU/GPU switching to avoid serialization and CUDA memory issues. An end-to-end visualization layer through Streamlit is provided with a comparison table of the models, score breakdown pie chart, attention heat map, and RAG retrieval display. Experiments show that the Flan-T5-XL strikes the best balance among interpretability, detail, and speed: faithfulness scores were between 0.86, coherence scores averaged 4.5/5, and the inference times were rather moderate (2-10} seconds) outperforming the other Flan-T5 family of models.

title: Distributed Generalized Distributionally Robust Equilibrium Seeking for Dynamical Games Under Unknown Time-Varying Interference
authors: Longcheng Liu, Shuai Liu, Yiguang Hong, Lihua Xie, Guangchen Wang
abstract: This paper investigates a distributed generalized Nash equilibrium-seeking problem in stochastic dynamical systems, focusing on two key challenges: 1) nonlinear coupled constraints and nonlinear dynamics, and 2) nonconvex objectives influenced by disturbances with unknown time-varying distributions. To address these challenges, a distributionally robust game framework with an exact penalty is proposed. We introduce a first-order equilibrium concept suitable for nonconvex-nons-mooth settings and ensure finite-sample guarantees. Furthermore, a distributed zeroth-order feedback algorithm is proposed to solve the problem. This algorithm utilizes gradient estimators for the objective functions and subgradient estimators for the exact penalty terms. We provide a detailed analysis of the relationship between communication errors and the dynamic energy of the system, along with an expected upper bound for the zeroth-order gradient estimation. Our findings indicate that the expectation of the time-accumulated regret grows at a sublinear rate. Furthermore, as the distribution stabilizes, we show that the empirical distribution converges with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$O(1)$</tex> sampling complexity.

title: Smart Pill Dispenser with Facial Recognition and Health Monitoring
authors: Ranjini R, Sabarigirinathan C, Sanjay V, H. Joseph Prabhakar Williams
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: With the increasing need for personalised healthcare and medication adherence, smart healthcare devices have become essential. This paper presents a Smart Pill Dispenser with Facial Recognition and Health Monitoring, designed to ensure accurate medication delivery and real-time patient monitoring. The system integrates facial recognition for patient authentication, a microcontroller (ESP32) for intelligent control, and sensor-based health monitoring to track vital signs such as heart rate, temperature, and blood pressure. Simulation and prototype testing confirm accurate medication dispensing, reliable patient identification, and continuous health monitoring. The proposed system demonstrates promise for enhancing medication adherence, patient safety, and remote healthcare management.

title: A Unified Modeling Framework for Cognitive Wildlife Monitoring
authors: P. Venkateswarlu Reddy, D. Ganesh, Sivakala Sridevi, Thupakula Venkata Srikala, Ukkusuri Arun Kumar, V. Yugesh
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Automatic identification of common species plays a crucial role in biodiversity monitoring, ecological research, and agricultural management. However, existing approaches often struggle with inter-class similarities, intraclass variability, and limited generalization across diverse datasets. To address these challenges, this work proposes an automatic species identification method based on ensemble learning integrated with deep learning. The proposed framework leverages multiple deep neural architectures to capture complementary feature representations and employs an ensemble strategy to improve classification robustness and accuracy. By combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with advanced ensemble techniques such as stacking and weighted voting, the model effectively mitigates individual model biases and enhances generalization across species categories. Experimental validation of image datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to single deep learning models with improvements in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

title: WirelessEV-OPT: Metaheuristic Optimization of Resonant Frequency and Power Transfer Using PSO and GWO
authors: Anupriya S, K. Malarkodi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Wireless electric vehicle (EV) charging has become one of the potential successful solutions to traditional plug-in charging systems, but its efficiency is highly influenced by the misalignment of the coils, resonant frequency error, and thermal variations. In order to solve these problems, this paper presents WirelessEV-OPT; a hybrid metaheuristic optimization model combining Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) to optimize power transfer efficiency and voltage stabilization in dynamic charging conditions. It has in place a comprehensive two-way wireless power transfer (WPT) model, which involves compensation networks, frequency restrictions and thermal limits. The hybrid algorithm PSO-GWO comes up with a multi-objective fitness criterion that combines coil alignment, resonant frequency selection as well as reduction of losses. Results of simulations show that simulation exhibits better convergence behavior and is more robust than single algorithm methods. WirelessEV-OPT is more efficient, less prone to misalignment, lower thermal spikes, and is more efficient in various loads. The next-generation wireless EV chargers infrastructure suggested has an optimization framework that is a scalable/intelligent togetherness that is capable of delivering a robust energy transfer in the face of real world uncertainties. The WirelessEV-OPT model is able to promote the sustainability of metropolitan mobility by using energy-efficient EV charging. Its wireless power transmission is optimized; it enhances transportation and urban energy infrastructure to encourage greener and smarter cities.

title: Synchronization Control Based on Sequential Convergence
authors: Huan Li, Shuangsi Xue, Hui Cao, Dongyu Li
abstract: Dear Editor, This letter focuses on the synchronization of system state components under the constraint of convergence sequence. Firstly, we introduce the predefined-sequence-synchronized control problem, a unique synchronization challenge where all state components must be synchronized to the origin following a predefined convergence sequence. A controller is proposed to solve the predefined-sequence-synchronized control problem in first-order affine systems, and the proposed controller can regulate the convergence sequence of state components by adjusting their ratios. Finally, the effectiveness of the theoretical findings is demonstrated through a numerical examnle.

title: Swin-ResU-Net: Hybrid Transformer CNN for Spatial Segmentation of Satellite Imagery
authors: Tejas Chauhan, Rajakumar K, Charan Rajana, Kaviti Sravan, Sai Bhargav Kasetty
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The resolution of satellite imagery has significantly improved due to the unprecedented growth in Earth observation technologies, necessitating the use of suitable and efficient semantic image segmentation techniques to perform spatial analyses. Most traditional CNN-based segmentation models fail to model long-range spatial dependencies and multiscale contextual relationships, which are inherent in satellite imagery. In this paper, the authors present a hybrid deep learning framework that combines the Swin Tiny Transformer with a ResNet50 backbone within a UNet-style architecture. The suggested model maintains spatial localisation through encoder-decoder skip connections and models global dependencies by utilising Swin Transformer's hierarchical selfattention mechanism. With the ResNet50 backbone, this enhances feature reuse by enabling precise gradient flow. The OpenEarthMap dataset, which featured a varied collection of globally dispersed land-cover samples, was then used to train and validate the model. With a peak validation IoU of 0.54 and an accuracy of 0.67, the experimental results show a progressive improvement over epochs, outperforming a number of baseline architectures in terms of both boundary precision and spatial consistency. The Swin-ResU-Net framework that was suggested brings together hierarchical transformer representations and convolutional feature learning so that the satellite imagery can be segmented spatially with precision. The results of the experiment show that the proposed method is not only robust but also generalizable, as it has consistently improved its performance across different evaluation metrics.

title: Implicit Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis: A Systematic Review
authors: Wenlong Jia, Jing Shan, Jiaying Wang, Zhang Mengyang
abstract: None

title: AsymDrive: Fingertip-Free Force Illusions Induced by Asymmetric Vibrations on Dorsal-Palmar Tendons
authors: Koki Fukuda, Kazuma Aoyama, Hideaki Kuzuoka, Takuji Narumi, Tomohiro Amemiya
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: Force feedback to the hand is essential for enhancing immersion in VR and facilitating skill transfer. However, no existing method allows unconstrained hand and arm positions while keeping the fingertips free. We present a novel fingertip-free technique that induces directional force illusions on the entire hand by applying asymmetric vibrations to antagonistic dorsal and palmar tendons. Through two user studies, we demonstrate that directional accuracy is significantly above chance level under dual-point asymmetric vibration, in contrast single-point or symmetric vibration. By integrating effective haptic feedback with unconstrained hand interaction, our approach offers a promising avenue toward richer VR experiences.

title:
A Study of the Scale Invariant Signal to Distortion Ratio in Speech Separation with Noisy References
                    <sup>*</sup>
authors: Simon Dahl Jepsen, Mads Græsbøll Christensen, Jesper Rindom Jensen
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: This paper examines the implications of using the Scale-Invariant Signal-to-Distortion Ratio (SI-SDR) as both evaluation and training objective in supervised speech separation, when the training references contain noise, as is the case with the de facto benchmark WSJ0-2Mix. A derivation of the SI-SDR with noisy references reveals that noise limits the achievable SI-SDR, or leads to undesired noise in the separated outputs. To address this, a method is proposed to enhance references and augment the mixtures with WHAM!, aiming to train models that avoid learning noisy references. Two models trained on these enhanced datasets are evaluated with the non-intrusive NISQA.v2 metric. Results show reduced noise in separated speech but suggest that processing references may introduce artefacts, limiting overall quality gains. Negative correlation is found between SI-SDR and perceived noisiness across models on the WSJ0-2Mix and Libri2Mix test sets, underlining the conclusion from the derivation.

title: Multiple Distributed Wardens with Amplify-and-Forward Fusing Strategy in Covert Communications
authors: Zhilin Chen, Jia Zhang, Xiaobo Zhou, Jiande Sun, Shihao Yan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Information Forensics and Security (WIFS)
abstract: This paper investigates covert wireless communication in the presence of multiple distributed wardens. In the proposed system model, a transmitter (Alice) aims to covertly send information to a legitimate receiver (Bob) with the aid of a friendly jammer, while multiple adversarial wardens (Willies) monitor the channel and forward their observations to a centralized fusion center (FC). The FC then performs a global decision to detect potential transmissions. To assess the covert performance of the system, we first derive the optimal decision threshold and the corresponding minimum decision error probability at the FC. We then propose an optimal amplification coefficient (OAC) scheme that maximizes detection uncertainty at the FC. Numerical results show that the OAC scheme achieves the lowest detection error probability, and its performance advantage becomes more prominent with an increasing number of wardens. Furthermore, both analytical and simulation results reveal a fundamental trade-off between covertness and transmission reliability, emphasizing the need for careful system-level design in multi-warden environments.

title: ASRU 2025 Preface
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)

title: ProtoCLAP – Prototypical Contrastive Language-Audio Pretraining
authors: Adria Mallol-Ragolta, Björn Schuller
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: We propose ProtoCLAP, a framework that integrates prototypical representations of the targeted classes in the languageaudio contrastive learning paradigm. Projecting the audio and the language representations in a shared embeddings space – where the prototypical representations are computed –, ProtoCLAP aims to maximise the similarity of the audio embeddings and their corresponding audio and language prototypes, while enforcing the similarity between both prototypical representations. We conduct our experiments on the MASCFLICHT Corpus and the Second DiCOVA Challenge Dataset. ProtoCLAP achieves the best results in three out of the six scenarios investigated. For face mask type and face mask coverage area recognition, ProtoCLAP scores the best Unweighted Average Recall on the test set, 62.8% and 56.7%, respectively. For COVID-19 detection, ProtoCLAP obtains the highest Area Under the Curve on the test set when exploiting the breathing sounds, 84.77%.

title: Record Replay Repeat: Improving Interactivity Between Non-Player Characters with Additive Record and Replay
authors: Klara Brandstätter, Benjamin J. Congdon, Anthony Steed
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: Non-player characters (NPCs) are a vital part of many 3D and virtual reality (VR) experiences, but animating them can be a time-consuming and costly process, often requiring expensive motion-capture setups, especially when tracking multiple people simultaneously. Single-user record and replay in VR enables a single user to animate multiple NPCs on their own, offering a cheaper and more convenient solution as the devices are consumer-grade and already track user movements. A limitation of single-user record and replay is that an early recorded character has no information about a later recorded character and might not be able to interact with them believably. We therefore study interactivity between NPCs as an emergent property when actors can see other characters in later recordings as opposed to acting primarily on their own in the first recording. We conducted a user study (N=144) where participants compared first, second, and third recording runs of different social scenarios with multiple NPCs. Our results suggest that for scenarios with low and medium interactivity where timing is not crucial and characters are not in close contact, a single run can be sufficient, whereas for high-interactivity scenarios, with close-contact group interactions, two recordings are more appropriate.

title: Fabrication of High-Resolution Digital Torque Wrenches With Quartz Crystal Force Sensors
authors: Cheng-Yi Wu, Qiushi Liu, Kun Li, Gangyin Ding, Jung-Chieh Chen, Jiahui Liang, Chin-Yin Chen, Chang-Chun Zhao, Min-Chiang Chao
abstract: A sensor head of quartz crystal resonant chips was developed as a force/torque sensor, incorporating parameters such as resolution, sensitivity, and response time. This sensor was applied to the torque detection technology of digital torque wrenches. The main body of the digital torque wrench was mechanically designed and simulated in advance to concentrate mechanical energy within the groove structure where the sensor head was installed. Compared with traditional strain gauge-based digital torque wrenches, which typically exhibit a measurement resolution of more than 1%-FS, the digital torque wrench utilising the quartz crystal force sensor head achieved a detection resolution of less than 0.2%-FS. Additionally, the reliability of the digital torque wrench was verified, and the results demonstrated its effectiveness.

title: Full V -band High-Efficiency Gap Waveguide-Based Single-Layer Corporate-Feed Slot Array Antenna
authors: Yi-Xiang Li, Bing-Zhong Wang, Lin Peng, Jun-Wei Dong
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents a single-layer corporate-feed 16 × 16-slot array antenna based on E-plane groove gap waveguide (E-GGW), which exhibits high antenna efficiency and an ultra-wide impedance bandwidth spanning the entire V-band (50-75 GHz). To facilitate a broadband 2 × 2-slot subarray, an ultra-wideband E-GGW 1-to-4 feeding network incorporating two types of novel unilaterally embedded septums (UESs) is proposed. These UESs not only ease manufacturing difficulty, enabling one-to-one excitation of radiating slots through a single-layer feeding network, thereby reducing processing cost and assembly complexity, but also grant the 2 × 2-slot subarray ultra-wideband capability. Moreover, for the purpose of constructing a large ultra-wideband corporate-feed network to excite the subarrays, two key ultra-wideband components are introduced: a compact E-GGW T-junction power divider and a novel WR-15 to E-GGW hybrid transition-divider with a UES. To verify the design, a prototype is fabricated and measured. The measured relative impedance bandwidth (S11 < -10 dB) is approximately 43.6% (49-76.3 GHz), fully covering the entire V-band. Within the same band, the measured average antenna efficiency exceeds 80%, and the peak antenna efficiency reaches 91%.

title: “Reflecting on Mark Weiser's Vision in 2026”
authors: John Tinnell

title: Edge Extension for Missing Anatomical Features: A Mask-Guided Spatial Diffusion Framework for Ultrasound Scoliosis Image Outpainting
authors: Chen Zhang, Wei Guo, De Yang, Weidong Cai, Yongping Zheng, Sai Ho Ling
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Accurate scoliosis diagnosis relies on precise spinal curvature measurement, traditionally using radiographic Cobb's angle. Ultrasound imaging offers a radiation-free alternative via ultrasound curve angle (UCA) estimation, but its clinical utility is limited by incomplete anatomical information due to the restricted field of view (FOV) during scanning. This hinders key tasks like segmentation and landmark detection, restricting ultrasound's broader adoption in scoliosis assessment. To address this challenge, we propose an edge-aware outpainting diffusion framework that restores missing spinal anatomy by integrating mask-guided spatial diffusion. Specifically, the model is trained to predict noise between randomly selected target windows and anchor regions using spatially encoded masks. During inference, a dedicated edge-preservation mechanism guides the generation of anatomical structures. In addition to mitigating hallucinations in diffusion-based generation and ensuring perceptual consistency between generated and retained regions, we incorporate a total variation loss to enforce structural smoothness and coherence across the entire output. This approach effectively constrains reconstruction within masked regions, improving the recovery of anatomical features—particularly in cases of complex S-shaped spinal deformities commonly seen in scoliosis ultrasound imaging, where the field of view is inherently limited. Extensive experiments demonstrate our approach achieves the lowest Fréchet Inception Distance (180.97) and highest Inception Score (1.87 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.12), while improving thoracic and lumbar UCA estimation accuracy by 47.8% and 24.6%, respectively. Thoracic structure detection also increases by 8.1% compared to Swin-Unet. Low KL divergence and Wasserstein distance confirm strong distributional alignment between generated and real anatomy. Overall, our framework enables anatomically consistent outpainting under limited FOV, enhancing ultrasound's reliability for clinical scoliosis assessment.

title: Dependency-Aware Microservice Deployment Optimization via Neural Heuristic Learning
authors: Hulin Nie, Xiangguo Zhao, Xin Bi, Xin Yao, Junjie Zhou, Ye Yuan
abstract: Microservice deployment should consider dependency constraints and resource requirements while minimizing deployment costs. There are various dependencies among microservices, and such intricate dependencies bring great difficulties to efficient deployment. Despite significant progress, existing deep learning-based methods are not expressive enough to capture the complexity of microservice dependencies. Meanwhile, metaheuristic methods encode such dependencies into problem-specific heuristics through explicit formulae, yet such formulations remain static and inherently limited. This paper addresses these issues by formulating the microservice deployment problem (MSDP) as a variable-sized bin packing problem. We first introduce a heterogeneous graph neural network to effectively represent the complex dependencies of MSDP. Based on this representation, we propose a novel reinforcement learning policy to design heuristics without the need to derive explicit formulae. Guided by the learned heuristics, we further devise a parallel ant colony optimization algorithm to accelerate solution construction. Experimental results from three representative microservice applications demonstrate that our method obtains near-optimal solutions while significantly outperforming the state-of-the-art methods in computational efficiency.

title: FD-SCU: Frequency Decomposition-based Spectrum Collaborative Upsampling for Point Cloud Color Attribute_supp1-3678090.pdf
authors: Hui Yuan
abstract: Existing point cloud color upsampling methods typically treat color upsampling as an interpolation problem within a local color or implicit feature domain. This largely overlooks the ability of the frequency domain to capture color correlations in local point sets. To address this limitation, we propose a spectrum collaborative strategy that uses frequency decomposition on voxel blocks (VBs) to enhance point cloud color reconstruction. We first voxelize the low-resolution (LR) color point cloud to generate multiple VBs and introduce a virtual filling strategy that adaptively assigns colors to empty voxels in each VB, ensuring that the irregularly distributed color information fully occupies the VB. We then apply the discrete cosine transform, known for its strong frequency-domain representation of locally smooth signals, to each color-filled VB to obtain frequency coefficients. These frequency coefficients are separated into high-frequency (HF) and low-frequency (LF) components. The LF coefficients, together with the LR color point cloud, are fed into a multi-scale cross-domain feature extraction module to capture deep features. Next, a Gaussian perturbation-based feature expansion generates upsampled color features, which are used to regress a coarse upsampled color point cloud. Finally, a high-frequency-guided residual refinement module uses the HF coefficients to refine the coarse upsampled result and produce a high-fidelity color point cloud. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our code will be publicly available at https://github.com/wangwenchaoxx/FD-SCU.

title: Real-Time Implementation and Experimental Validation of a Novel Vector Control Strategy for a PV-Fed Reduced-Structure Inverter–Driven Induction Motor Using dSPACE 1104
authors: Imen Nouira El Badsi, Bassem El Badsi
abstract: Photovoltaic (PV)-fed induction motor drives are increasingly used in low-power applications, yet conventional three-phase inverters often impose high hardware complexity and cost. This paper presents a novel vector control strategy specifically tailored for reduced-structure B3 delta inverters supplying induction motors in PV systems. The proposed approach combines Rotor Field-Oriented Control (RFOC) with advanced modulation techniques, ensuring fast torque response, low harmonic distortion, and improved flux regulation despite the limited voltage vectors of the delta inverter. A real-time implementation on a dSPACE 1104 platform is conducted, complemented by a full experimental test bench, validating both steady-state and dynamic performance. Results demonstrate that the reduced-structure inverter, when paired with the proposed control, achieves performance comparable to conventional B6 inverters while offering significant hardware simplification, cost reduction, and enhanced compactness. This study provides practical insights into the design and deployment of efficient, low-cost PV-fed induction motor drives for emerging renewable-energy applications.

title: Unified Object Detector with Dynamic Switch Mechanism Based on Deep Learning Advances: A Paradigm-Aware Architecture for Adaptive Detection
authors: Oussama Hmidani, El Mehdi Ismaili Alaoui
abstract: Object detection research has evolved through different architectural paradigms, including two-stage region-based models, one-stage efficiency-focused detectors, and more recent transformer methods, where each performs well in a specific domain but none is optimal overall. This fragmentation requires static architectural decisions by developers and often leads to suboptimal performance when application requirements change. In this work, we address these limitations through two complementary and novel contributions. First, we conduct a systematic bibliometric analysis of the object detection literature from 2014–2025 to derive an empirical paradigm–domain relationship matrix, enabling the identification of the most influential detection paradigms together with their dominant application domains. This provides, for the first time, a data-driven mapping between detection paradigms and domain-specific requirements. Second, we propose a novel Unified Object Detector with Dynamic Switch Mechanism (UOD-DSM), which operationalizes the insights obtained from the bibliometric analysis into an adaptive detection architecture. The UOD-DSM integrates multiple detection paradigms within a unified framework and employs a context-aware switching mechanism to dynamically select the most suitable paradigm at runtime according to operational constraints and input characteristics. Experimental validation demonstrates that the proposed framework offers improved flexibility and robust performance across diverse applications, including real-time video analysis and precision-demanding medical imaging, while outperforming conventional static detectors in cross-domain evaluation. These results demonstrate the potential of the proposed approach to advance object detection toward fully adaptive vision systems capable of aligning architectural strategies with varying operational contexts.

title: Model Predictive Control of DAB Converters for RMS Inductor Current Minimization Under ZVS Constraints
authors: Nguyen Ngoc Nam, Young Il Lee
abstract: This paper introduces a model predictive control (MPC) method for dual active bridge (DAB) converters, which minimizes the root mean square (RMS) inductor current while ensuring zero-voltage switching (ZVS). First, the power losses that reduce DAB efficiency are analyzed. It is revealed that achieving optimal efficiency requires both minimizing the RMS inductor current and maintaining ZVS, thereby reducing switching, conduction, and magnetic component losses. Subsequently, an expression for the RMS inductor current is derived, which involves two decision variables: the inter-phase and outer-phase shifts. By formulating and solving an optimization problem that minimizes the RMS inductor current under ZVS constraint, the steady-state solutions for the inner and outer phase shifts are determined. To achieve fast-tracking performance and eliminate steady-state error, the MPC approach is designed based on the output filter. Additionally, Lyapunov stability theory is employed to guarantee system stability. The steady-state inner phase shift solution is used to satisfy the ZVS constraints, while the final outer-phase shift is determined based on the MPC approach and inter-phase steady-state solution, ensuring power transmission. To validate and highlight the effectiveness of the proposed method, extensive hardware experiments are conducted and compared with recent studies.

title: Excluded Volume Effect in GI/G/s Queues
authors: Takako Hoshiyama, Katsuhiro Nishinari
abstract: This study developed an approximation framework for GI/G/s queues with excluded volumes. Building on a discrete-time GI/G/1 framework that embeds the totally asymmetric simple exclusion process (TASEP) via matrix-geometric and aggregation methods, we derived a Sakasegawa-type approximation with an effective service rate μ=μpe capturing the exclusion-induced delay. A gamma-based moment approximation links the Yanagisawa M/M/1 system with excluded volume (E-Queue) and the Alfa model for GI/G/1 queues by matching the first two moments of geometric and gamma distributions, yielding an internal density ρmean that is fed back into the Sakasegawa formula as a response coefficient in a two-stage correction scheme. For numerical validation, we used the Alfa model and an aggregated Alfa model for GI/PH/s queues as references, and evaluated it over ranges of squared coefficients of variation, utilization, server counts, and service-time distributions. Within the validated region (ca2≥1,cs2≤1,s≤4,h=0.1) (consistent with our previous study), the relative mean queue-length error is typically below 10%. Additional tests for exponential, Erlang, Weibull, Coxian-2, and static service times show full-range errors against the Alfa reference below 1% for light-tailed gamma-type cases and within a few percent for the more variable Coxian and Stacy cases, with mid-range traffic percentiles largely below 1%. We quantified the information loss from temporal discretization via Riemann sum approximations of gamma distributions and related it to the approximation error. Overall, the framework provides a data-assisted explicit approximation formula GI/G/s with a gamma-based two-stage correction and an error analysis that links the excluded volume.

title: Semantic Similarity-Based AI RMF and NIST SP 800-53 Integrated Crosswalk Structured Process
authors: Yu-Rae Song, Jin Kwak
abstract: The rapid advancement of AI and digital technologies has introduced complex cyber threats. To manage these threats effectively, the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) has evolved its Risk Management Framework (RMF), culminating in the 2023 release of the AI RMF 1.0 to address AI-specific risks. However, NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 does not fully encompass inherent AI risk factors such as bias and lack of explainability, necessitating the integration of AI-specific risk management into existing security frameworks. While organizations like stackArmor have conducted crosswalk research, previous studies often lack quantitative validation and methodological precision. In this study, we propose a hybrid automated crosswalk framework that integrates Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) and Sentence-BERT to systematically map security controls to AI RMF functions. The methodology follows a two-stage structured process: first, deriving unique keywords for each control family using context-enhanced embeddings and Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) to minimize redundancy; and second, employing a hybrid query structure incorporating domain-specific keywords—such as model drift and distributed denial of service (DDoS) against machine learning (ML) infrastructure—to ensure technical granularity. Our approach demonstrated 98.63% consistency with expert-defined mappings and identified 60% more valid controls compared to manual baselines. These findings suggest that the proposed framework significantly enhances the scalability and precision of AI risk compliance, providing a robust foundation for automated, high-fidelity security auditing.

title: Connected Vehicular Network Ground-to-Air Target Tracking Based on Bearings Measurements: Pseudolinear Information-Guided Strategy
authors: Zhihao Xiao, Luyao Zhang, Ning Liu, Tong Guo, Luhan Jin, Yao Mao
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: This paper investigates ground-to-air Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) tracking using bearing-only line-of-sight (LOS) measurements provided by electro-optical tracking systems (ETS) mounted on a connected vehicular network. The key challenges arise from asymmetric mobility constraints and limited observability in three-dimensional space. To address these issues, a closed-loop tracking framework is proposed that integrates state estimation with ground vehicle coordination. A distributed pseudolinear filter is developed to handle the nonlinear nature of bearing-only observations and improve estimation stability in a decentralized setting. On top of this, an information-theoretic guidance strategy is formulated by adapting the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) to ground-to-air sensing geometry, enabling explicit quantification of the relationship between sensor positioning and estimation accuracy. The framework employs Voronoi-based spatial decomposition to achieve scalable coordination among multiple ground vehicles, balancing estimation precision with spatial coverage. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method enhances tracking accuracy and improves target observability, offering a practical solution for UAV tracking under realistic conditions.

title: ZipVoice: Fast and High-Quality Zero-Shot Text-to-Speech with Flow Matching
authors: Han Zhu, Wei Kang, Zengwei Yao, Liyong Guo, Fangjun Kuang, Zhaoqing Li, Weiji Zhuang, Long Lin, Daniel Povey
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Existing large-scale zero-shot text-to-speech (TTS) models deliver high speech quality but suffer from slow inference speeds due to massive parameters. To address this issue, this paper introduces ZipVoice, a high-quality flow-matching-based zero-shot TTS model with a compact model size and fast inference speed. Key designs include: 1) a Zipformer-based vector field estimator to maintain adequate modeling capabilities under constrained size; 2) Average upsampling-based initial speech-text alignment and Zipformer-based text encoder to improve speech intelligibility; 3) A flow distillation method to reduce sampling steps and eliminate the inference overhead associated with classifier-free guidance. Experiments on 100 k hours multilingual datasets show that ZipVoice matches state-of-the-art models in speech quality, while being 3 times smaller and up to 30 times faster than a DiT-based flow-matching baseline. Codes, model checkpoints and demo samples are publicly available.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>https://github.com/k2-fsa/ZipVoice

title: Multilingual Dataset Integration Strategies for Robust Audio Deepfake Detection: A SAFE Challenge System
authors: Hashim Ali, Surya Subramani, Nithin Sai Adupa, Lekha Bollinani, Sali El-Loh, Hafiz Malik
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: The SAFE Challenge evaluates synthetic speech detection across three tasks: unmodified audio, processed audio with compression artifacts, and laundered audio designed to evade detection. We systematically explore self-supervised learning (SSL) front-ends, training data compositions, and audio length configurations for robust deepfake detection. Our AASIST-based approach incorporates WavLM large frontend with RawBoost augmentation, trained on a multilingual dataset of 256,600 samples spanning 9 languages and over 70 TTS systems from Codec-Fake, MLAAD v5, SpoofCeleb, Famous Figures, and MAILABS. Through extensive experimentation with different SSL frontends, three training data versions, and two audio lengths, we achieved second place in both Task 1 (unmodified audio detection) and Task 3 (laundered audio detection), demonstrating strong generalization and robustness.

title: Neuromorphic Adaptive Precision RISC-V Processor with Real-Time Precision Scaling and Neuronal State Management
authors: Om Maheshwari, Sahil Maurya, Bikram Paul
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Fixed-precision arithmetic units in conventional processors consume unnecessary energy when full computational width is not required. This paper presents a novel neuromorphic adaptive precision RISC-V core that dynamically selects execution width (16 bit, 32 bit, or 64 bit) at runtime using a bio-inspired controller with neuronal state management. The microarchitecture introduces: (i) Pack/Unpack Units (PUU_IN/PUU_OUT) that maintain RV32 architectural semantics while enabling internal 64 bit precision scaling, (ii) a four-lane reconfigurable sliced ALU operating as 4×16, 2×32, or 1×64 configurations via precision tags, and (iii) Control/Status Registers (CSRs) providing software configurability and runtime observability. The neuromorphic controller employs hysteresis and refractory mechanisms to prevent oscillation while optimizing energy-accuracy trade-offs. Our Zybo Z7 FPGA prototype validates functional correctness and demonstrates measurable energy savings of 12–15% across representative benchmarks with less than 1% performance overhead, while maintaining complete ISA compatibility.

title: Linear Order Graph Sparsification for MCMC and Mean-Field Annealing
authors: Debraj Banerjee, Santanu Mahapatra, Kunal N. Chaudhury
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: We tackle the problem of running Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) and Mean-Field (MF) annealing on very large Ising graphs by introducing a deterministic Greedy-Pruning graph sparsification method. The goal is to cut down computation while keeping solution quality high. Our algorithm does this by pruning edges to create sparse subgraphs with limited node degree, using an easy-to-compute bound on a worst-case spectral measure. A GPU implementation builds these sparse matrices efficiently and speeds up the annealing updates. Tests on Ising models show big drops in per-iteration cost with almost no loss in accuracy. Compared with the recent Copy Node method, our approach is more stable and robust. By combining fast sparse-matrix generation with multi-GPU, block-based matrix-vector multiplication, we scale to Ising systems with about 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sup> spins and nearly half a billion couplings. These results show that graph sparsification can be a practical and scalable tool for large-scale optimization and statistical physics.

title: Solving the Number Partitioning Problem with Hardware-Accelerated Probabilistic Computing
authors: Kiran Magar, Utsav Banerjee
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Probabilistic computing is a quantum-inspired paradigm which can efficiently solve computationally challenging problems. In this work we present a probabilistic computing hardware accelerator for solving the Number Partitioning Problem (NPP) with 1024 fully connected p-bits implemented in an AMD Virtex UltraScale+ FPGA. We also propose a methodology to systematically derive critical algorithm hyper-parameters for fast and accurate convergence. Our accelerator successfully solves NPP instances with problem sizes up to 1000 and consistently reaches the best known solution while achieving accuracy and performance comparable to state-of-the-art.

title: GPU Kernel-Level Characterization and Optimization of Transformer Models: From Baseline to LoRA Fine-Tuning
authors: Sasi Snigdha Yadavalli, Badrinath Ramamurthy
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: This study addresses the problem of systematically applying hardware-software optimizations to standard transformer training pipelines. The investigation examines how hardware-aware GPU techniques and LoRA hyperparameters jointly influence computational efficiency and model quality. By profiling CUDA kernel-level metrics and model-level scores (BLEU, chrF), we demonstrate how optimizations like FlashAttention and batched memory operations reduce execution time, while LoRA hyperparameters affect both model quality and computational load.

title: Comparison on Power Cycling Test Consistency Across Technical Routes: Core Stress-Relative Parameters Alignment as the Key to Equivalence
authors: Maoyang Pan, Erping Deng, Yanghai Wei, Guiqin Chang, Wangjun Zhou, Jie Chen, Hongyu Sun, Yuxing Yan, Hao Liu, Yongzhang Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Power cycling test (PCT) is one of the most significant tests to evaluate the long-term reliability of power semiconductor devices. The accuracy and consistency of PCT results are fundamental to the reliability assessment and lifetime prediction. However, the emergence of diverse technical routes from different manufacturers for PCT equipment on the market has raised critical concerns regarding measurement consistency and result comparability. This study presents a systematic investigation into the equivalence of distinct technical routes and test benches from three aspects of the tested power semiconductor devices: the steady-state operational parameters, transient thermal behavior, and the PCT lifetime. It is revealed that equipment based on different technical approaches achieves consistent measurement of both steady-state parameters and transient thermal characteristics at the same conditions. And then the lifetime should be consistent according to the lifetime models. Furthermore, it is demonstrated closely aligned PCT lifetime distributions across different test benches, with Weibull mean lifetime differing by merely 3% under the identical core stress-relative parameters. The findings confirm that PCT results are governed by the core stress-relative parameters rather than by the specific test equipment or ancillary adjustment parameters. In practical applications, this work helps to develop a fundamental methodology that shifts the focus from equipment-specific validation to stress-state alignment, providing a practical framework for harmonizing test protocols across different laboratories and equipment vendors.

title: An EEG-Based Multi-Source Domain Knowledge Transfer Framework for Cross-Session and Cross-Subject Emotion Recognition
authors: Xiaoya Zhu, Haohao Zheng, Xiaolong Chen, Hong Zeng, Chenpu Li, Motonobu Hattori, Xiang Zhang
abstract: Multi-source domain adaptation (MSDA) aims to transfer knowledge from multi-source domains to an unlabeled target domain, making it highly suitable for cross-session and cross-subject emotion recognition based on electroencephalography (EEG). Nevertheless, current MSDA models primarily focus on the relationship between the global feature distribution of the source and the target domain while neglecting the relationship between local feature pairs, resulting in poor robustness. In this study, we propose an EEG-based Multi-Source Domain Knowledge Transfer Framework (MS-KTF) for cross-session and cross-subject emotion recognition. The MS-KTF uses a triplet loss combined with a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) to achieve unbiased alignment at both the domain and data-pair levels of each pair data in the source and target domains. Moreover, the introduction of knowledge distillation enables MS-KTF to achieve better robustness without requiring additional training time. We evaluated the MS-KTF's performance on the DEAP, SEED and SEED-IV datasets for two-, three- and four-class emotion recognition, respectively. The experimental results demonstrated that MS-KTF achieves competitive performance in both cross-session and cross-subject scenarios.

title: Integrating a Degradation-Driven Methodology in Board Level Vibration Testing
authors: V. Thukral, R. Roucou, F. Xu, M. Van Soestbergen, J. Costa, R.T.H. Rongen, R. Jordans, W.D. Van Driel, G.Q. Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: The global drive towards intelligent autonomy in safety-critical electronics is accelerating the demand for zero-failure strategies. It is intensifying the need for real-time monitoring and prediction of failures in advance. Dynamic stresses encountered in actual operating environments, such as mechanical vibrations can introduce unknown reliability risks, particularly at the solder–PCB interface. One of the reasons these risks remain unknown is the absence of established industry standard for assessing board level reliability under vibration loading. This work combines physical damage sensors embedded in the components (also known as intelligent automation), artificial intelligence (AI), and finite element simulation. A four-wire resistance circuit enables in-situ tracking of electrical degradation, capturing early indicators of damage progression. This approach is demonstrated on Ball Grid Array (BGA) packages and is compared to the Quad-Flat-No-Leads (QFN) packages. Then, a neural forecasting model is applied to predict resistance evolution and estimate remaining useful life. Finite element simulations are used to establish the relationship between resistance change and crack length, thereby validating the experimental data. The method is extendable to other electronic packages and supports predictive maintenance through enhanced understanding of degradation under real-world conditions.

title: Full-Duplex-Bench: A Benchmark to Evaluate Full-Duplex Spoken Dialogue Models on Turn-taking Capabilities
authors: Guan-Ting Lin, Jiachen Lian, Tingle Li, Qirui Wang, Gopala Anumanchipalli, Alexander H. Liu, Hung-Yi Lee
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Spoken dialogue modeling poses challenges beyond text-based language modeling, requiring real-time interaction, turn-taking, and backchanneling. While most Spoken Dialogue Models (SDMs) operate in half-duplex mode—processing one turn at a time—emerging full-duplex SDMs can listen and speak simultaneously, enabling more natural conversations. However, current evaluations remain limited, focusing mainly on turn-based metrics or coarse corpus-level analyses. To address this, we introduce Full-Duplex-Bench, a benchmark that systematically evaluates key interactive behaviors: pause handling, backchanneling, turn-taking, and interruption management. Our framework uses automatic metrics for consistent, reproducible assessment and provides a fair, fast evaluation setup. By releasing our benchmark and code, we aim to advance spoken dialogue modeling and foster the development of more natural and engaging SDMs.

title: Voice Factor Control Using FIR-Based Fast Neural Vocoder for Speech Generation Applications
authors: Yamato Ohtani, Takuma Okamoto, Tomoki Toda, Hisashi Kawai
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: We have proposed a fast neural vocoder based on the source-filter model introducing finite impulse response (FIR) filters called FIRNet. FIRNet is highly compatible with digital signal processing (DSP) and can, therefore, generate waveforms from vocoder parameters and modified voice factors, such as tone, intonation, and timbre, using DSP. Although modern neural waveform generation systems, such as voice conversion and text-to-speech (TTS), have been able to generate human-like synthetic speech and imitate the reference speaker’s timbre, it is challenging for these systems to manually control arbitrary voice factors, unlike traditional TTS systems. By applying FIRNet to modern neural waveform generation systems, they can achieve arbitrary voice factor controllability. We will demonstrate two applications using FIRNet with DSP-based voice factor controls: one is analysis-synthesis, and the other is text-to-speech.

title: GL-MambaLite: A Lightweight Global-Local Feature Enhanced Network for Building Extraction
authors: Jiabin Liu, Yibo Wang, Huaigang Huang, Zhen Li, Dakang Wang, Xiankun Yang, Jinnian Wang, Peijun Du
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: As a key element of human settlements and fundamental features in maps, buildings play a critical role in urban planning and environmental monitoring. Automatically extracting buildings from remote sensing images is of significant practical value. However, accurately extracting buildings is challenging due to their diverse scales, complex geometries, and spectral similarities to backgrounds. To effectively capture fine-grained structural details and long-range spatial dependencies, current deep learning methods mainly rely on complex hybrid architectures combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Transformer-based models. Although these methods achieve high accuracy, their substantial computational burden limits their scalability, making them impractical for large-scale building mapping tasks. In addition, while sophisticated multi-scale feature extraction modules and fusion strategies help accommodate scale variations, their redundant parameters and elaborate structures further exacerbate the computational demands. Consequently, balancing building extraction accuracy and operational efficiency remains a critical challenge for practical deployment, particularly in resource-constrained scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose a lightweight global-local feature enhancement network, GL-MambaLite, for building extraction. Specifically, we construct a lightweight Global-Local Feature Enhancement (GLFE) block that combines Mamba and CNN to simultaneously capture global dependencies and local details. Furthermore, to mitigate complex background disturbances, we design a lightweight Parallel Multi-scale Feature Extraction block (PMFE) based on depth-wise convolution, enabling the efficient aggregation of multi-scale features. Moreover, we introduce a Holistic Grouped Aggregation strategy to facilitate direct interaction between semantic and detailed features, effectively reducing redundancy while preserving geometric integrity. Extensive experiments on the WHU, Inria, and custom UAV building datasets demonstrate the superiority of GL-MambaLite. Experimental results demonstrate that GL-MambaLite achieves the highest IoU scores across all three datasets, recording 90.38% on the WHU dataset, 79.71% on the Inria dataset, and 84.65% on the custom UAV dataset. With an inference speed of 593.79 FPS on 512×512 inputs, GL-MambaLite establishes an optimal equilibrium between accuracy and efficiency. Compared to the state-of-the-art Transformer-based model (SegFormer), GL-MambaLite reduces computational complexity (FLOPs) by 42.8% and improves inference speed by 52.2%, while also achieving a 7.5 times speedup over the recent Mamba-based baseline (RS3Mamba). Ablation studies confirm the architectural efficacy of our proposed components. The code will be freely available at https://github.com/ljb-1/GL-MambaLite-A-lightweight-global-local-feature-enhanced-network-for-building-extraction.

title: Double Dynamic Event-Triggered Leader-Following Consensus for Nonlinear Multiagent Systems Under Directed Graphs
authors: Lili Wang, Shiming Chen
abstract: This article gives an investigation to the leader-following consensus issue for Lipschitz nonlinear multiagent systems. A double dynamic event-triggered control scheme has been proposed to reduce unnecessary triggering behaviors and thus save resources. This scheme consists of two independent dynamic event triggering mechanisms: one controls communication between agents, and the other determines controller updates. To avoid Zeno behavior, a constant has been added to the triggering mechanism. Moreover, a simplified parameter selection procedure is proposed that eliminates the need for verification of matrix inequalities. In addition, this work was explored in a directed network consisting of only one spanning tree. Finally, the correctness of the presented control strategy is verified through a simulation example.
