title: VGG16 and ResNet50 for Potato Leaf Disease Prediction
authors: Animesh Srivastava, Sumant Kumar Mohapatra, Parveen Kumar, Bhupender Singh Rawat, Rakesh Pandey, Vikash Sawan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Precision potato leaf disease detection is a key element in ensuring food security and minimal loss of food production. Traditional detection using manual processing has the limitation of high cost and labor intensity in particular during the infection stage. In this paper, we have done a comparison analysis transfer learning based performance of two popular deep CNN architectures- VGG16 and ResNet50 to classify potato leaf diseases. The data set was normalized and enhanced and then categorized into healthy, early blight, and late blight. Custom dense layers are added and further trained both models on the augmented dataset to evaluate their performance. Two different architectures are tested in the experiments. Results show that both models can achieve exciting performance results, in particular ResNet50 has the best performance; the average accuracy of ResNet50 is 96%, which is 2% higher than VGG16. With ResNet50, residual learning architecture can alleviate the problem of vanishing gradient and learn features more effectively, thus it can achieve better generalization results and faster convergence speed. The results indicate that transfer learning with deep residual architectures allows for a, robust and scalable model to detect potato diseases. This work is a stepping stone for intelligent, IoTfriendly and agriculture systems for disease detection and yield improvement of crops.

title: Comparative Model Analysis for Early Detection of Breast Cancer Using Clinical Data
authors: Bukke Deekshith Naik, Vasala Hema, Rimmalapudi Ravichandra, Mude Nagarjuna Naik, Sriramkumar R, Lakshmanan M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Breast cancer detection is one of the prominent topics in medical research, as timely detection of it may help in obtaining a better treatment outcome and thus a better survival. This paper presents a comparative study of four machine learning algorithms, including Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), and the Naive Bayes algorithm, for the effective classification of breast cancer. The original data was effectively processed and missing values invalidated, and important features were normalized to obtain a well-trained model. Each of these algorithms is evaluated by means of various metrics of evaluation, including precision, recall, and F measure, which helps to understand the predictive capability of the model for each class of the model. Our experimental analysis shows that Logistic regression gives a superior class performance and also class stability, making it an appropriate algorithm for application in medical diagnosis. The results also affirm the usefulness of machine learning in practical life so that the peculiarities of illness do not permit diagnostic errors, and hospitalizations may be minimized by early intervention. This research leads up to the great mass of information put into form on methods of treatment of cancer detection through data analysis, and a field of development is opened out for future work in hybrid integration and methods of feature selection with better methods of predicting.

title: Golang Scheduler
authors: Egor Levchuk, Denis Pushkar, Denis Vasin
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: Golang-Scheduler is a conceptual study of a hybrid, priority-aware scheduler designed for Go’s goroutine model. We first diagnose weaknesses of the default Go scheduler when serving heterogeneous and latency-sensitive workloads—issues that manifest as elevated tail latency, steal contention, and suboptimal handling of mixed CPU/IO tasks. Based on this analysis, we propose a compact, runtime-compatible design that augments per-P run queues with short-lived priority buckets, applies priority-aware work-stealing, and uses cooperative yielding together with adaptive backoff to mitigate contention. Rather than delivering a full runtime patch, we present a precise architectural specification and implementation principles, and we evaluate the idea with discrete-event simulations and a lightweight user-space prototype. Our evaluation indicates meaningful benefits in target scenarios—up to 35% reduction in 99th-percentile latency and up to 20% throughput improvement under mixed CPU/IO loads - while preserving Go semantics. We conclude with sensitivity analysis, deployment considerations, and recommended next steps for practical adoption.

title: Multi-Modal Explainable AI Predictions for Dermatological Disease and Allergy Diagnosis
authors: Britney Spierce G, Rajeswari M, Stewart Kirubakaran S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Millions of people around the world have suffered from skin diseases, and a lot of morbidity has resulted from delays or incorrect diagnoses. Dermatological diagnosis traditionally is based on subjective visual judgment by experts, which usually faces accessibility issues. This work proposes an AI-assisted framework for multi-class skin disease diagnosis that is enhanced with Explainable Artificial Intelligence for enhancing the trust, interpretability, and clinical usability of the developed model. The proposed system uses three deep learning architectures: ResNet50, DenseNet121, and a hybrid ResNet50 integrated with the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), to extract highly discriminative visual cues by focusing on relevant lesion areas both spatially and channel-wise. A curated dataset of dermoscopic skin lesion images has been used; several preprocessing and augmentation techniques like flipping, rotation, and color jittering were utilized to enhance robustness against diverse clinical imaging conditions. Weighted Random Sampling and class balanced loss are applied to mitigate challenges offered by class imbalance. Grad-CAM-based heatmaps provide clear visual explanations through highlighting of the disease specific lesion regions, strengthening clinical transparency. Also, it integrates the complete diagnostic workflow into one web interface with FastAPI, providing image upload, symptom-aware evaluation, differential diagnosis, and personalized food recommendations according to the predicted condition. The results affirm that the combination of attention enhancement, feature level interpretability, and calibrated prediction will improve the diagnostic accuracy and clinical trustworthiness of AI algorithms by leaps and bounds. Experimental evaluation has established that the proposed Hybrid ResNet50 + CBAM model achieves the topmost performance with 89 % accuracy, 87 % precision, 86 % recall, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 6. 5 \%}$</tex> F1-score, outperforming the baseline models ResNet50 and DenseNet121.

title: AI Driven Predictive Maintenance for Single Phase Induction Motor
authors: Kanthi Raghu Vardhan, Lingappa. J, Pagindla Nithin, Anishetti Ruchitha, Kalagotla Chenchireddy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Predictive maintenance has become a crucial approach for monitoring industrial equipment, leveraging Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) to anticipate failures before they occur. This paper presents an AI-driven predictive maintenance system designed for singlephase and three-phase induction motors. The proposed system employs a Random Forest classifier trained on a structured dataset comprising key motor operating parameters such as voltage, current, temperature, vibration, runtime, and motor type. A web-based user interface, developed using Gradio, enables real-time health assessment and provides maintenance scheduling recommendations. The model achieves a perfect classification accuracy of 1.0 on the test dataset, demonstrating its strong predictive capability and reliability. The paper discusses the complete workflow, including dataset preparation, model training, system design, evaluation metrics, and considerations for practical deployment.

title: Energy Consumption Forecasting in Smart Homes: A Scalable Machine Learning Approach
authors: Kintali Nikhilesh, Rohan Paul, S Rajat Pandey, Gouthaman P, T Preethiya
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The paper presents the methods used for smart home Energy Consumption Forecasting, which utilizes distributed computing with PySpark and Machine Learning algorithms. PySpark distributed computing technologies were utilized to process one million records of energy consumption, drawn from various datasets. The four machine learning algorithms were used, such as Random Forest, XGBoost, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM). Random Forest implementation achieved a 0.4319 kWh Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and a 76.48% prediction accuracy and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}=0.7648$</tex>, which shows a production-ready performance. PySpark is applied in distributed feature engineering, MLlib in scalable machine learning, and operational deployment in realtime. The results demonstrate that these techniques can manage a massive amount of data while it can preserve high prediction accuracy, which will provide benefits for Smart Home Applications.

title: A Novel Finite-Time Stability Criterion for Nonlinear Systems Involving Flexible Delayed Impulses
authors: Shuchen Wu, Xiaodi Li, Shiji Song
abstract: Dear Editor, This letter studies the finite-time stability (FTS) problem of nonlinear impulsive systems with delayed impulses. The system under consideration is subjected to a flexible class of time-varying delays in impulsive signals, allowing them to arbitrarily span one or more impulse instants. Sufficient Lyapunov-based conditions for FTS of such systems are presented, where a novel class of impulse time sequences is established for all admissible uncertainties of delayed impulses. Compared with the existing FTS studies with delayed impulses, our proposed results not only provide greater versatility for impulse delay, but also relax the constraint on the uniformity of impulse intervals. It also shows that delayed impulses have a potential negative impact on system stability by weakening the effect of impulsive control or even converting a stabilizing influence into a destabilizing one. Finally, we give an example with corresponding numerical simulations to validate our theoretical results.

title: Ladder-DenseNet Driven Deep Transfer Learning Framework for Automated Glaucoma Detection Using Fundus Images
authors: A. Thamodharan, R. Pugalenthi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The reason of glaucoma being identified early in the world is one of the common causes of irreversible blindness even though once the eye suffers irreversible damage, the eye may not be able to take in any further information and vision becomes blurred. Most of the classical CNN based models are also ineffective in the representation of multi-scale features and the strong gradient flow which leads to poor classification results in real clinical practice. To defeat them, the proposed study will use a Ladder-DenseNet-based deep transfer learning framework in the diagnosis and classification of glaucoma disease by using fundus images. The main strength of the work is the refining of features towards the ladder-like, and the closely connected convolutional layers, which offered the opportunity to provide the information further and reuse the features more efficiently, as well as localize the glaucoma-related features in the structure. The originality of the proposed solution based on the idea is the ability to learn high-level representations and simultaneously reduce the loss of features and improve the stability of the model compared to the classic deep learning setup. The components of the workflow are preprocessing, Ladder-DenseNet based transfer learning, feature improvement, and final classification of the glaucomatous and the normal retina images. It was evaluated on the Kaggle Glaucoma Fundus Image dataset where it is demonstrated that the proposed model works better with an accuracy of 98.3, precision of 97.9, recall of 97.5, F1-score of 97.7, specificity of 98.1, AUC of 99.0 and Cohen kappa of 97.1.

title: Smart AI Multimodal Architecture for Automated Emergency Detection and Alerts
authors: T G Manjunath, Zainab Imtiyaz, Bokka Sowjanya, Varshini K S, Sharanya Bengoor
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The increasing demand for intelligent and proactive emergency response systems exposes the limitations of conventional surveillance setups that rely on manual monitoring and are prone to delayed or missed distress detection. This study presents a multimodal AI-based emergency alert system that integrates audio and video analysis for real-time distress recognition. The audio module processes incoming signals through a structured pipeline involving audio pre-processing with Librosa for resampling and normalization, feature extraction using YAMNet embeddings pretrained on Google AudioSet, and binary scream classification through a lightweight feedforward neural network optimized with Adam. Out of 699 total samples, 511 non-scream and 152 scream samples were correctly identified, achieving an overall classification accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 5 \%}$</tex>. The video module detects dynamic “Help Me” hand gestures through frame preprocessing, Mediapipe landmark extraction, and sequential modeling with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, achieving a classification accuracy of approximately 80 % while operating under varying lighting conditions, motion blur, and camera angles. Outputs from both modules are fused in an integration and alert layer using Twilio, ensuring rapid, scalable, and reliable emergency detection in high-risk environments.

title: Placement Prediction Model Using Neural Prophet and AdaBoost
authors: Nagendra Reddy B, Thota Balaji, M Venkata Pavan Kumar, Anusuri Krishna Veni, K Swetha, K Suresh Babu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: In today's changing job market, predicting campus placements has become more complex due to several unpredictable global trends. Educational institutions need accurate models that can reflect these changes to better prepare their students for employment. This research proposes a hybrid placement prediction model that combines Neural Prophet and AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting) algorithms to forecast placement outcomes with improved reliability. Unlike earlier models that considered static or limited global variables such as recession, overhiring, and automation, the proposed model includes three new dynamic factors—AI disruption, cost-cutting, and industry downsizing—which strongly influence hiring trends in the technology sector. Neural Prophet (NP) helps in identifying timebased variations and long-term market shifts, while AdaBoost strengthens the classification accuracy by reducing errors through adaptive learning. Experimental results on historical placement data demonstrate that the hybrid NP and AdaBoost model achieves a prediction accuracy of 95.9%. The findings show that dynamic industrial factors have a measurable impact on student employability, and integrating them into prediction systems significantly improves performance. This work contributes a reliable and adaptable framework for academic institutions to forecast placement probabilities and make informed strategic decisions for student career readiness.

title: PCAD: A Raspberry Pi Zero–Based Portable Cybersecurity Platform with On-Device K-Means Modeling of Wi-Fi Environments
authors: Ali Mucedidi, Mohammed Al-Hubaishi, Mirna Tout
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Portable, low-power platforms for cybersecurity are valuable in classrooms, field audits, and authorized research. We present the Portable Cybersecurity Analysis Device (PCAD), a self-contained Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W system with an integrated LCD and hardware buttons that unifies wireless AP management, network reconnaissance, device enumeration, traffic capture, and offline peer-to-peer messaging behind a menu-driven ondevice interface. Beyond classical tooling, PCAD now includes a lightweight K-Means clustering pipeline that summarizes local Wi-Fi scans (e.g., network count, average/max RSSI) and partitions observations into signal-quality clusters to characterize radio environments directly on device. In field trials, the system remained interactive while performing essential tasks; under controlled stress loads, UI latency increased but remained usable, host discovery slowed modestly, and service enumeration accuracy degraded slightly while capture rate and CPU utilization rose as expected on the constrained platform. Collectively, these results show that a tightly integrated, ethics-aligned embedded platform can support both hands-on cybersecurity workflows and ondevice ML analytics for environment assessment without external terminals or cloud inference. We release a modular design that supports reproducibility and extension to intrusion-detection experiments on higher-capacity boards.

title: Adaptive Ai Traffic Signal Control
authors: Ms. T. Bhuvaneswari, M. Anushree, M. Arunkumar, M. Dhivyaa, V. Hariharan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The predictability regarding the vehicle flow pattern, congestion pattern, and scalability compared to the fixed timer traffic signals makes the jobs of traffic managers more complex. Currently available traffic solutions based on AI are mostly hardware intensive and based on PCE or are black boxes in terms of logic regarding traffic signal control and do not provide reasons behind other decision-making choices. The increase in cost of operation and absence of clarity pertaining to factors like the above are hindrances in accepting this system. Adaptive AI Traffic Signal Control the proposed project will seek to achieve the implementation of the adaptive software-only realtime traffic flow control system with the advantage of being transparent, flexible, and fully automated. At the structure level of such an adaptive system, the YOLOv8 detection model will be employed for the interpretation of images pertaining to cars, buses, trucks, and motorbikes for up to four lanes when conducting calculations depending on the number of vehicles. Traffic lanes will be set with a relative density level established with the use of special logic for ranking. The clean interface from Flask provides users with access to image uploads from traffic signals with marked detection results, comparison of lane density, and finally the analysis of how AI assigns traffic signal times. The engine of the simulation works through the cyclic display of traffic signals in each lane based on the length of the green time period. The user can follow the system process towards the end in the stages of the procedure from detection up to traffic signals. The experimental results of proposed method achieve High Accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$92 \%, \text{Reliability} 90\%, \text{Adaptability} 91\%, \text{Efficiency} 90\%$</tex>.

title: Teachers’ and Students’ Perceptions of the Role of AI Tutors in Engineering Study Programs
authors: Egor A. Chebotarev, Sergey O. Shaposhnikov
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: The paper discusses the role of AI tutors in improving the quality of engineering education. It analyzes the results of student and faculty surveys on the role of AI tutors in teaching and learning. A conclusion is drawn regarding the desired roles and functions of AI tutors in engineering education.

title: On Decimations of M-Sequences Giving Few Valued Cross-Correlations
authors: Serdar Boztaş, Ferruh Özbudak, Elif Kurtaran Özbudak, Eda Tekin
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: In this paper, we report some experimental results on decimations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d$</tex> which yield three-valued cross-correlations for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$m$</tex> - sequences over odd prime fields <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex>. First, we performed a search for decimations different from the ones mentioned by Helleseth in his 2014 survey [6] of open problems on crosscorrelation. We have performed extensive computations but have not found any cases that are inequivalent to those stated as Open Problem 2 in [6]. In this context, we conjecture the form of the decimations that result in these three-valued crosscorrelations and leave the proof as an open problem. We also report computational results on a class of sequences related to Open Problem 8 from Helleseth and Li's [7] 2024 paper. Specifically, we investigate the case <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n \equiv 2(\bmod 4)$</tex>, which was explicitly excluded from the original problem. Based on our findings, we propose a new conjecture on the correlation distribution for this particular family, thus formulating a new, related open problem.

title: DSA-LoRA: A Seamless Content-Style LoRA Fusion for Adversarial Sample Generation
authors: Gangtao Han, Xinhai Zhong, Lingling Li, Song Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Synthetic images generated by diffusion models pose cybersecurity risks through misinformation and fraud. To tackle these risks, personalized image generation, which involves synthesizing a new image with the content of a source image and the style of a target image, provides sufficient attack samples that are able to strengthen the anti-counterfeiting capacity of the detection models through adversarial training. Current personalized image generation research typically fuses the content and style extracted from sample images to produce high-quality synthesized images. However, such a fusion is likely to generate incoherent visual results, leading to a severe lack of fidelity. In this paper, we propose DSA-LoRA, a diffusion-step-aware low-rank adaptations (LoRA) integration method, that merges trained content and style LoRAs to generate coherent visual images. By analyzing the diffusion process, we find that the early steps are primarily responsible for the subject reconstruction, while the later ones steadily move towards enhancing the visual appearance of the generated images. This finding motivates us to design a piecewise integration strategy to enable a seamless transition from content reconstruction to style enhancement in personalized image generation. To effectively fuse the information contained in the original content and style LoRAs, Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is employed in this paper to identify the suitable fusion positions for LoRAs. The experimental results demonstrate that DSA-LoRA generates coherent and natural results, outperforming state-ofthe-art methods in terms of content and style fidelity.

title: Intelligent Assessment System Enforcing Structured Data Integrity for Real Time E Learning
authors: Mohammed Faisal, K. S. Shashikala, Nabil Khan, Nihal L, Mohammed Faizan Ajani, Yukthi S Mohan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The Intelligent Assessment System (IAS) is a powerful, full stack web platform designed for the total automation of alternative assessments such as quizzes and to eliminate workloads. When a teacher enters unstructured PDF or DOCX course material through a secure web interface the regulated data pipeline starts instantly creating a unique, shareable quiz link. Crucially, the core uses of Pydantic Schemas is to ensure complete data integrity and system stability which is actually a common problem in LLM integration. This procedure actually resolves the LLM data structuring issue prior to persistence by rigorously requiring a verifiable JSON output from the Native OpenAI API. For effective, real time relational scoring and permanent submission for record keeping this consistently structured assessment data is passed through a SQLAlchemy Data Pipeline. The output is a full, event-driven score report that includes feedback and explanations for each quiz question. It is sent right away via SMTP. The IAS sets up a very reliable and scalable system for making verifiable educational data assets which greatly helps modern data-driven educational systems.

title: Datasets, Metrics, Benchmarks and Future Research in Autonomous Driving: A Review
authors: Yuchen Li, Siyu Teng, Zizhang Wu, Junhui Wang, Mingyu Liu, Zhe Xuanyuan, Long Chen
abstract: Data-driven autonomous driving is a hot topic in academic and industry research due to its impressive performance, flexible mobility, and reduced human intervention. However, the development of this technology relies heavily on large datasets that contain accurately annotated data, obtained through artificial or semi-automated strategies. Consequently, datasets play a crucial role in autonomous driving, and their characteristics significantly impact the effectiveness of algorithms. Currently, there are several diverse datasets available, such as KITTI and CityScape, that cover various tasks. However, researchers often overlook the unique features, similarities, and specificities of these datasets. Furthermore, to the best of our knowledge, there is a lack of survey articles focusing on special metrics and benchmark performance on different datasets in autonomous driving. Therefore, the purpose of this article is to analyze autonomous driving datasets, guide researchers on collecting and utilizing relevant datasets, summarize evaluation strategies, analyze benchmark performance, and provide future research points to enrich the autonomous driving community. We believe that this work will assist researchers in evaluating their data using suitable metrics and offer a fresh perspective on autonomous driving.

title: VRCL-MSVFF: An Accurate Multi-Source Visual Feature Fusion-Based Vehicle–Road Collaborative Localization Method for Indoor Testing Scenarios
authors: Yicheng Li, Wei Zhong, Yingfeng Cai, Hai Wang, Zhixiong Li
abstract: Accurate localization of intelligent vehicles in testing scenarios is crucial for verifying whether they can accurately drive, recognize, and respond to various situations in the testing environment. In indoor testing scenarios, satellite localization methods completely fail. Vehicle-mounted sensor localization methods cannot effectively cope with the environmental changes. Indoor wireless communication localization methods are affected by NLOS, failing to meet the accuracy requirements for localization. For the indoor testing scenario of intelligent vehicles, this paper proposes a low-cost roadside localization method to replace the current mainstream vehicle localization methods. Only vehicle-mounted beacons and roadside monocular cameras were used. First, a multi-camera array calibration technology based on Gaussian Bayes theory was used to calibrate the camera array in the testing field. Second, improvements were made to YOLOV9 for indoor testing scenarios, aiming to improve the recognition and accuracy of roadside cameras on vehicle-mounted beacons, and then a spatial localization scheme is applied to the roadside cameras on the basis of deep learning beacon recognition and by integrating multi-source beacon recognition information. We constructed a dataset of vehicle-mounted beacons and conducted ablation experiments on the improved beacon recognition algorithm. The mAP@0.5 of the model increased by 3.2%. Localization experiments were conducted on the first floor of the Automotive Engineering Research Institute of Jiangsu University and the underground parking lot of the Leisi Building of Jiangsu University. The average localization errors were 5.9 and 6.4 cm, respectively. The success and accuracy of localization were evaluated, the average success is 98%, and the average accuracy is 85.9%. The experiments showed that the proposed method achieved good localization results in different testing scenarios.

title: Scalable machine learning-based approaches for energy saving in densely deployed Open RAN
authors: Xuanyu Liang, Ahmed Al-Tahmeesschi, Swarna Chetty, Cicek Cavdar, Berk Canberk, Hamed Ahmadi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: Densely deployed base stations are responsible for the majority of the energy consumed in Radio access network (RAN). While these deployments are crucial to deliver the required data rate in busy hours of the day, the network can save energy by switching some of them to sleep mode and maintain the coverage and quality of service with the other ones. Benefiting from the flexibility provided by the Open RAN in embedding machine learning (ML) in network operations, in this work we propose Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based energy saving solutions. Firstly we propose 3 different DRL-based methods in the form of xApps which control the Active/Sleep mode of up to 6 radio units (RUs) from Near Real time RAN Intelligent Controller (RIC). We also propose a further scalable federated DRL-based solution with an aggregator as an rApp in None Real time RIC and local agents as xApps. Our simulation results present the convergence of the proposed methods. We also compare the performance of our federated DRL across three layouts spanning 6–24 RUs and 500–1000m regions, including a composite multi-region scenario. The results show that our proposed federated TD3 algorithm achieves up to 43.75% faster convergence, more than 50% network energy saving and 37. 4% lower training energy versus centralized baselines, while maintaining the quality of service and improving the robustness of the policy.

title: In Situ Grown MoS₂ on FTO: A Scalable and Biocompatible Platform for Selective Detection of Dopamine
authors: Christeena Thomas, K A Benazeera Beegum, Saranya Sasi, S Sreeja, A S Asha, R Reshmi
abstract: 2D Molybdenum disulfide ( MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>) has emerged as a pivotal material in nanoscience and nanotechnology due to its remarkable properties and multifaceted applications. The primary obstacles that need to be tackled in applications like electrochemical sensing utilizing 2D MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> are its limited scalability and poor adhesion. Consequently, there is strong motivation to design sensing platforms that balance selectivity, stability, and practical feasibility in real-world sensing applications. This work addresses these critical material challenges while placing equal emphasis on establishing a stable and selective platform for dopamine sensing, which remains a crucial objective in neurochemical diagnostics. The study adopts a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis approach to grow highly adhesive MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> on the FTO substrate, a first-time report of utilizing such MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/FTO electrode for stable, selective, and repeatable detection of dopamine. The material has shown very good crystallinity, high surface area, greater electrocatalytic performance, and excellent hemocompatibility and biocompatibility with 98.63% cell viability even after 24 hours. The sensing performance of the MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/FTO electrode was studied using Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV), and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS). Dopamine molecules were detected in the linear range of 0.5 to 15 μM (R2= 0.98) with a limit of detection (LoD) of 93 nM. The sensitivity of the electrode is observed to be 0.82 μAμM⁻¹mm⁻², and the selectivity study of dopamine in the presence of major interfering species shows that the MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/FTO electrode is only selective to dopamine. The significance and novelty of this work lie in its comprehensive approach to overcoming key limitations associated with MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-based electrochemical sensors, particularly issues of scalability and electrode adhesion. By simultaneously focusing on achieving high selectivity and reproducibility for dopamine detection, the study contributes meaningfully to the development of reliable electrochemical platforms for sensing applications. Furthermore, the repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of the fabricated material have been evaluated to ensure reliability and practical applicability.

title: Robert K. Brayton and the Theory of Nonlinear Circuits [In Memoriam]
authors: Wolfgang Mathis
abstract: This article is dedicated to the scientific achievements of Robert K. Brayton in the field of nonlinear circuit theory, who passed away in January 2025. Internationally, he is recognized as an expert and pioneer in the fields of logic synthesis and formal verification as well as circuit simulation, but his groundbreaking work on nonlinear RLC circuits is rarely mentioned. Therefore, following a biographical sketch of Robert K. Brayton’s scientific career, his contributions to nonlinear circuits in the context of the applications that formed the basis of his research will be explained. In particular, we discuss the influence of his research results on the further development of the field of nonlinear circuits. Based on relevant literature, it is shown that the differential geometric interpretation of the dynamics of electrical circuits leads to a physical model for a broader class of physical systems, which is also of interest in control theory and physics.

title: Contact Resistance Measurements in Two-Layer Spiral-Coated-Conductor Cable
authors: Guangwei Xu, Yuya Koyama, Yusuke Sogabe, Naoyuki Amemiya
abstract: We experimentally measured the tape-to-tape contact resistance distribution in two-layer spiral-coated-conductor cables. Current was injected at one end of the outer layer and extracted from the opposite end of the inner layer. In this case, the entire contact resistance of the cable can be measured using a simple four probe method, based on the voltage between the two coated conductors and current. Besides, we measured the current in each layer, distributed along the sample, using arrays of Hall sensors installed in the core of the cable. By combining the voltage between layers and the layer currents distributed along the cable, we determined the local contact resistance distributed along the cable. We also studied the influence of several fabrication conditions on contact resistance. Three samples were fabricated for each condition to investigate the variability.

title: SuperLink 150 m Demo and Large-Scale Perspective
authors: D. Willén, M. Pitzer, C. Thidemann, O. Holte, J. Kunert, C. Kozaris, C. Wolff, W. Prusseit, R. Bach, W. T. B. de Sousa, M. Noe, P. Michalek, R. Prinz, A. Alekseev
abstract: The The SuperLink project has developed a 110 kV, 500 MVA high-temperature superconducting (HTS) cable system for urban grid reinforcement. It aims to provide a commercially attractive and compact HTS cable solution. A full-scale 150 m demonstration system was installed and operated for 6 months in Munich, Germany, showcasing the cable's reliability, compactness, and performance. The cable features intrinsic compensation for axial thermal contraction, low AC loss, and minimal cryostat heat inleak. This paper presents the technical development, qualification testing, the cooling configuration and operational results such as coolant temperatures at various locations within the cable loop during a variety of load scenarios, steady currents, pulses and daily load cycles. Finally, it discusses the implications for large-scale deployment in urban grids as determined by simulation of the full 110 kV city-wide grid of Munich.

title: Direct Target Localization for Distributed Hybrid Active-Passive Radars with Direct-path Interference
authors: Qiyu Zhou, Ye Yuan, Wei Yi, Hongbin Li, Maria Sabrina Greco, Fulvio Gini
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Distributed hybrid active-passive radars (HAPRs) with separately deployed transmitters and receivers face localization performance limitations due to the inevitable presence of direct-path interference (DPI). To improve target localization accuracy and enhance DPI suppression, we develop a direct position estimator tailored for stationary/low-speed targets in distributed HAPRs, which jointly exploits signals from both active transmitters and non-cooperative illuminators of op portunity (IOs). However, the joint processing of active and passive observations, coupled with the unknown signals from non-cooperative IOs, leads to a intractable high-dimensional optimization problem. The expectation-maximization algorithm is employed to address the optimization problem with low complexity, iteratively suppressing DPI and updating the position estimate. To further reduce computational complexity, we use a sequential estimation method that decomposes the high dimensional problem into a sequence of low-dimensional ones. Wealso derive the Cram´ er–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) to provide an accurate performance benchmark for the proposed estimator. Extensive numerical analysis shows that the proposed direct estimator achieves estimation accuracy closer to the CRLB than estimators used in only active or passive distributed radars.

title: Evaluation of Visual Feedback Techniques for Mid-Air Teleportation in an Immersive Environment
authors: Nanjia Wang, Saadman Rahman, Brody Wells, Daniel Zielasko, Frank Maurer
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: Target selection-based teleportation is one of the most prevalent locomotion techniques in immersive virtual environments, mitigating visual motion sickness and overcoming physical space constraints. However, the conventional Point & Teleport method, widely implemented in commercial VR systems, restricts navigation to planar surfaces, limiting exploration of mid-air positions essential for tasks such as obtaining elevated viewpoints or bypassing obstacles. Mid-air teleportation extends the Point & Teleport metaphor to support free navigation in three-dimensional space, yet the influence of visual feedback on its performance and usability remains underexplored. This paper investigates six different implementations of visual feedback techniques during target specification in mid-air teleportation. We evaluated these techniques through a user study with 56 participants. The results reveal trade-offs between the downward-facing camera, which achieves the highest accuracy, and other visual feedback techniques that rely on occlusion cues, such as the spherical pointer, the spherical boundary, the ground reference cylinder, and the avatar, which result in lower task completion time. We propose initial design guidelines for target selection-based mid-air teleportation based on our findings.

title: Integrating Research into High Performance Computing Education via Particle-in-Cell Simulations
authors: Libin Varghese, Ayushi Sharma, Bhaskar Chaudhury
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Integrating active research themes into HPC education provides an effective way to engage students with real-world scientific challenges while deepening their understanding of performance engineering. This paper presents a recent initiative that connects an advanced undergraduate HPC course with ongoing research in computational plasma physics through a research driven laboratory assignment based on the Particle-in-Cell algorithm. Building on the CS301: High Performance Computing course at DA-IICT, DAU and the Let’s HPC pedagogical framework, a customized assignment was designed where students parallelized and optimized a computationally intensive PIC charge-deposition (scatter point-to-mesh interpolation) kernel. A dedicated webpage provided PIC background with visualizations, simulation animations, baseline C code, benchmark datasets, and literature resources. As part of the evaluation, the performance of the parallel codes submitted by the students was assessed across three computing systems. The overall evaluation revealed three performance based learning tiers - basic, intermediate, and advanced involving basic OpenMP parallelization to advanced optimizations, highlighting how progressively layered optimization strategies correlate with conceptual maturity. This new activity in the course improved student engagement, learning outcomes, research motivation, and understanding of scalability and performance trade offs. Overall, the approach aligns with Bloom’s higher cognitive levels (Analyze, Evaluate, Create) by blending research with hands-on problem solving in HPC.

title: Unifying Diarization, Separation, and ASR with Multi-Speaker Encoder
authors: Muhammad Shakeel, Yui Sudo, Yifan Peng, Chyi-Jiunn Lin, Shinji Watanabe
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: This paper presents a unified multi-speaker encoder (UME), a novel architecture that jointly learns representations for speaker diarization (SD), speech separation (SS), and multi-speaker automatic speech recognition (ASR) tasks using a shared speech foundational encoder. We leverage the hidden representations from multiple layers of UME as a residual weighted-sum encoding (RWSE) to effectively use information from different semantic levels, contributing to bottom-up alignment between tasks. This joint training approach captures the inherent inter-dependencies among the tasks, enhancing overall performance on overlapping speech data. Our evaluations demonstrate that UME substantially improves over the single-task baselines dedicated to SD, SS, and multi-speaker ASR on LibriMix evaluation sets. Notably, for SD, UME outperforms the previous studies, achieving diarization error rates of 1.37% and 2.29% on Libri2Mix and Libri3Mix evaluation sets, respectively.

title: Speech in-context learning of paralinguistic tasks
authors: Jeremy H. M. Wong, Muhammad Huzaifah, Nancy F. Chen, Ai Ti Aw
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: In-context learning adapts a large language model to a new task, without computationally expensive parameter updates. This has previously been demonstrated for text tasks, as well as speech tasks that rely primarily on lexical information, such as recognition and translation. This paper proposes to extend this investigation to consider the ability of current open-source models to exhibit in-context learning on speech tasks that require an understanding of paralinguistic information. The tasks of stutter detection, pronunciation assessment, and speech emotion recognition are investigated. The results suggest that current open-source models already exhibit some degree of speech in-context learning on paralinguistic tasks. To more fully utilise available adaptation data, it is also proposed to overcome the finite number of in-context exemplars allowed by the model’s prompt length limit, through ensemble combination over multiple in-context learning runs that each use different exemplars.

title: Hardware-Software Co-Design of Post-Quantum FALCON Digital Signature Scheme with RISC-V
authors: Rishabh Shrivastava, Utsav Banerjee
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: FALCON is a lattice-based digital signature scheme recently selected as NIST post-quantum cryptography standard. FALCON is well known for its fast verification support along with small key and signature sizes. However, implementation of the FALCON signature algorithm in resource-constrained edge computing systems is challenging due to the requirement of complex hashing, floating point arithmetic and polynomial transformation computations. In this work, we demonstrate resource-efficient hardware acceleration of FALCON signature generation and verification by performing hardware-software co-design of its computationally expensive functions with a light-weight 32-bit Vex RISC-V processor core with accompanying custom instructions. We propose Custom Function Units compatible with Vex RISC-V to accelerate Keccak permutations and polynomial transformations, along with a constant-time implementation of IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic. Our design achieves ≈ 4.3× speedup for signature verification and ≈ 1.6× speedup for signature generation compared to the baseline software on Vex RISC-V for both parameter sets of FALCON, when implemented on AMD Xilinx UltraScale+ FPGA. We also present an efficient verification-only variant of our design suitable for resource-constrained applications.

title: FlexCTC: GPU-powered CTC Beam Decoding With Advanced Contextual Abilities
authors: Lilit Grigoryan, Vladimir Bataev, Nikolay Karpov, Andrei Andrusenko, Vitaly Lavrukhin, Boris Ginsburg
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: While beam search improves speech recognition quality over greedy decoding, standard implementations are slow, often sequential, and CPU-bound. To fully leverage modern hardware capabilities, we present a novel open-source FlexCTC toolkit for fully GPU-based beam decoding, designed for Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) models. Developed entirely in Python and PyTorch, it offers a fast, user-friendly, and extensible alternative to traditional C++, CUDA, or WFST-based decoders. The toolkit features a high-performance, fully batched GPU implementation with eliminated CPU-GPU synchronization and minimized kernel launch overhead via CUDA Graphs. It also supports advanced contextualization techniques, including GPU-powered N-gram language model fusion and phrase-level boosting. These features enable accurate and efficient decoding, making them suitable for both research and production use.

title: Performance Evaluation of AI Inference on the Tenstorrent Wormhole RISC-V Accelerator
authors: Kishor Yerol Damodaran, Abhishek Patel, Krishan Gopal Gupta, Shashank Sharma, Samrit Kumar Maity, Sanjay Wandhekar
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: The growing computational demands of large-scale AI inference have shifted performance bottlenecks from algorithms to hardware, underscoring the need for systematic evaluation of accelerator efficiency. Consequently, architectural analysis of performance, scalability, and energy efficiency is critical for sustainable AI deployment. Existing evaluations predominantly target proprietary GPU and TPU platforms, leaving limited insight into open and customizable alternatives. This study presents a cross-domain performance evaluation of the RISC-V–based Tenstorrent Wormhole n300 across vision (ResNet-50) and language (Llama-1B–8B, Qwen-7B) inference workloads. Experiments were analyzed across four quantitative metrics: throughput, latency, scaling efficiency, and energy efficiency. The vision model achieved 6.3× and 4.9× higher throughput than the Grayskull e150 and RTX A4000, respectively, while the language model achieved comparable GPU-class throughput at one-third the power, delivering 3.5× higher throughput-per-cost than the H100 PCIe GPU. These findings demonstrate that RISC-V accelerators can deliver competitive and cost-efficient inference performance, while highlighting the importance of software-stack maturity for broader adoption in HPC AI workloads.

title: Non-Autoregressive Multi-Speaker ASR with Decoupled Speaker Change Detection
authors: Yingke Zhu, Lahiru Samarakoon
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Existing multi-speaker automatic speech recognition (ASR) approaches predominantly rely on autoregressive (AR) decoding. The AR decoding complicates the parallel computation. In this work we explore non-autoregressive (NAR) models for multi-speaker ASR to achieve faster inference. Multi-speaker ASR comprises two primary tasks: speech recognition and speaker change detection (SCD). We introduce a framework that decouples these tasks into separate branches. Inference is carried out in two stages: ASR decoding is performed first, then SCD decoding leverages the ASR output and predicts all the speaker changes in one pass. Experiments show that with NAR decoding, the proposed model outperforms the baseline by a relative cpWER reduction of 30% on LibriSpeechMix and 8% on AMI. Additionally, in scenarios with a 10% overlap ratio, our NAR model achieves a threefold increase in decoding speed with only a 1.2 % reduction in accuracy compared to a strong AR baseline.

title: Efficient Deployment of Large Speech Recognition Models on GPU
authors: Yuekai Zhang, Shuang Yu, Junjie Lai
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Large automatic speech recognition (ASR) models have achieved remarkable progress in recent years, but their deployment in production faces significant challenges due to large model sizes and autoregressive decoding methods. This paper presents comprehensive solutions for efficiently deploying large ASR models on GPUs using NVIDIA Triton Inference Server and TensorRT-LLM. Our deployment framework supports both encoder-decoder architectures and speech LLMs. With a modular design based on NVIDIA Triton, the framework can be easily extended to other model architectures. We implement optimized TensorRT-LLM engines for Whisper models and speech LLMs. Compared to existing implementations, our Whisper TensorRTLLM solution achieves more than $\mathbf{5 0 \%}$ throughput improvement. The complete deployment solutions are open-sourced and provide one-click deployment through docker-compose, facilitating rapid adoption in production environments. <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup> <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>https://github.com/k2-fsa/sherpa/tree/master/triton/whisper<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>https://github.com/k2-fsa/sherpa/tree/master/triton/speech_llm

title: AdaBit-TasNet: Speech Separation with Inference Adaptable Precision
authors: Mohamed Elminshawi, Srikanth Raj Chetupalli, Emanuël A. P. Habets
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Deploying advanced neural network-based speech separation (SS) models on resource-constrained devices is challenging due to their high computational and memory demands. Conventional network compression techniques, such as pruning and quantization, can alleviate these demands without significantly compromising performance. However, they lack the flexibility to select the compression factor at run-time to suit varying operating conditions, such as changing computational and energy budgets in battery-powered devices. In this paper, we introduce AdaBit-TasNet, an adaptable-precision network (APN) for SS that enables flexible bit-width selection during inference. Experimental evaluation on the Libri2Mix dataset demonstrates that AdaBit-TasNet achieves comparable performance to that of individually trained fixed-precision networks at several bitwidths.

title: Towards Efficient Speech-Text Jointly Decoding within One Speech Language Model
authors: Haibin Wu, Yuxuan Hu, Ruchao Fan, Xiaofei Wang, Kenichi Kumatani, Bo Ren, Jianwei Yu, Heng Lu, Lijuan Wang, Yao Qian, Jinyu Li
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Speech language models (Speech LMs) enable end-to-end speech-text modeling within a single model, offering a promising direction for spoken dialogue systems. The choice of speech-text jointly decoding paradigm plays a critical role in performance, efficiency, and alignment quality. In this work, we systematically compare representative joint speech-text decoding strategies—including the interleaved, and parallel generation paradigms—under a controlled experimental setup using the same base language model, speech tokenizer and training data. Our results show that the interleaved approach achieves the best alignment. However it suffers from slow inference due to long token sequence length. To address this, we propose a novel early-stop interleaved (ESI) pattern that not only significantly accelerates decoding but also yields slightly better performance. Additionally, we curate high-quality question answering (QA) datasets to further improve speech QA performance.

title: Multi-Distillation from Speech and Music Representation Models
authors: Jui-Chiang Wei, Yi-Cheng Lin, Fabian Ritter-Gutierrez, Hung-Yi Lee
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Real-world audio often mixes speech and music, yet models typically handle only one domain. This paper introduces a multi-teacher distillation framework that unifies speech and music models into a single one while significantly reducing model size. Our approach leverages the strengths of domain-specific teacher models, such as HuBERT for speech and MERT for music, and explores various strategies to balance both domains. Experiments across diverse tasks demonstrate that our model matches the performance of domain-specific models, showing the effectiveness of cross-domain distillation. Additionally, we conduct few-shot learning experiments, highlighting the need for general models in real-world scenarios where labeled data is limited. Our results show that our model not only performs on par with specialized models but also outperforms them in few-shot scenarios, proving that a cross-domain approach is essential and effective for diverse tasks with limited data. Code and models are released at https://github.com/johnwei0325/Multi-Distillation-from-Speech-and-Music-Representation-Models

title: scMSAC Assigns Single-Cell Multi-Omics Data At the Multi-Modal Cluster Via Subgraph Attention Autoencoder
authors: Jing Wang, Cheng Yang, Weijie Cai, Dayu Tan, Yun Ding, Junfeng Xia, Yansen Su, Chunhou Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Single-cell multi-omics sequencing represents an advanced technology capable of simultaneously measuring multiple omics data from the same cell. The joint clustering of single-cell multi-omics sequencing data enables a comprehensive depiction of cell states and uncovers intricate molecular mechanisms, holding immense significance in fields such as oncology, neurology, and developmental biology. However, the disparities in feature spaces across different omics layers and data noise present substantial challenges for achieving accurate clustering. To tackle these challenges, we introduce a novel clustering method for single-cell multi-omics data, termed scMSAC, which is grounded in a denoising subgraph attention autoencoder. The proposed method employs a weighted nearest neighbor graph strategy to ascertain the weights of multi-omics data, subsequently generating a similarity graph that holistically encapsulates intercellular connections through the weighted amalgamation of diverse omics perspectives. The scMSAC model captures the topological features of cells through the subgraph attention autoencoder, constructing relationships among cells. For the omics features extracted by the subgraph attention autoencoder, scMSAC incorporates an SCA (Spatial Channel Attention) mechanism for feature fusion to reduce the differences in feature spaces of different omics and achieve better clustering performance. Comparative experiments with various existing methods demonstrate that scMSAC has excellent clustering performance and performs well in detecting rare cell types and differential expression analysis. Our scMSAC model codes are freely available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/yancy2024/scMSAC</uri>.

title: Protecting Personal Space in Social Virtual Worlds: The Impact of Proximity-Control Features on Adoption Intention
authors: Runjie Xie, Florian Franke, Sandra Birnstiel, Benedikt Morschheuser
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: Harassment and personal space invasions have been a core privacy threat in social virtual worlds from the outset, often causing lasting harm to victims and reducing their willingness to engage with these technologies. Media coverage of such incidents further amplifies fear and discourages potential users. In response, platforms have increasingly emphasized proximity-control features that allow users to protect themselves both preventively and reactively – most prominently through safety bubbles and avatar blocking. However, no study to date has examined whether these features influence adoption-relevant factors. To address this gap, we conducted a video vignette-based online survey with 195 participants in Germany, each exposed to a mild personal space invasion scenario under one of three conditions: no safety features, safety bubble, or avatar blocking. Results show that both safety bubbles and avatar blocking positively influence relevant adoption factors such as trust, controllability, satisfaction, and use intention. Safety bubbles additionally improved perceived safety and reliability, appearing to be the more effective solution due to their preventive nature. Finally, implications of the findings are discussed.

title: A Unified Hand and Gesture Tracking via Offloading Framework for Object-mediated Interaction in Wearable AR
authors: Woojin Cho, Taewook Ha, Taejun Son, Woontack Woo
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: We propose a novel object-mediated hand interaction system that enables real-time operation with everyday objects on wearable augmented reality (AR) devices. Despite recent advances, both commercial and academic hand interaction techniques remain constrained, typically requiring external hardware or depending exclusively on bare-hand gestures. Motivated by these constraints, we developed an offloading framework that integrates a high-fidelity transformer-based 3D hand reconstruction model with a dynamic gesture recognition network powered by gated recurrent units (GRU). This architecture ensures stable and accurate gesture recognition even during interaction with physical objects. To evaluate its quantitative performance, we collected a custom dataset based on a predefined gesture set, achieving 93.0% accuracy in 5-fold cross-validation. The complete system implemented on Microsoft HoloLens 2 operates at a real-time framerate, and we further analyze the latency of each step in our framework. Through this interaction paradigm, users can experience immersive and intuitive AR in everyday environments with minimal disruption to natural action behavior. Our projects are available at https://github.com/kaist-uvrlab/UnifiedHOInteraction.

title: Introduction to the 1st International Workshop on RISC-V for HPC (RISCV-HPC)
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)

title: Evaluating the Viability of Additive Models to Predict Task Completion Time for 3D Interactions in Augmented Reality
authors: Logan Lane, Ibrahim Tahmid, Feiyu Lu, Doug A. Bowman
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: Additive models of interaction performance, such as the Keystroke-Level Model (KLM), are tools that allow designers to compare and optimize the performance of user interfaces by summing the predicted times for the atomic components of a specific interaction to predict the total time it would take to complete that interaction. There has been extensive work in creating such additive models for 2D interfaces, but this approach has rarely been explored for 3D user interfaces. We propose a KLM-style additive model, based on existing atomic task models in the literature, to predict task completion time for 3D interaction tasks. We performed two studies to evaluate the feasibility of this approach across multiple input modalities, with one study using a simple menu selection task and the other a more complex manipulation task. We found that several of the models from the literature predicted actual task performance with less than 20% error in both the menu selection and manipulation study. Overall, we found that additive models can predict both absolute and relative performance of input modalities with reasonable accuracy.

title: Heavy-Duty Gas Turbine Compressor Health Monitoring by Improved Closed-Form Continuous-Time Neural Network
authors: Enjian Chang, Congzhi Huang, Shuzhi Sam Ge
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Health monitoring plays a vital role in preserving the reliability of heavy-duty gas turbines. Accurate prediction of compressor performance in heavy-duty gas turbines is essential for enabling effective predictive health monitoring. However, existing compressor health monitoring approaches often neglect the continuous-time physical relationships within the compressor and exhibit limited robustness under multi-condition operation, resulting in reduced accuracy in degradation tracking. The focus of this study is to construct a performance model that captures nonlinear compressor dynamics characteristics across varying conditions, while providing physically meaningful indicators for fouling assessment. A compressor health monitoring method by improved closed-form continuous-time neural network is proposed. Firstly, correlation analysis is employed to select physically relevant feature variables. Subsequently, an adaptive gating module is then integrated into the closed-form continuous-time neural network to enhance its ability to model nonlinear dynamic behaviors across multiple operating conditions. Finally, a compressor fouling index based on the isentropic efficiency derived from model outputs is proposed to achieve health assessment. The effectiveness of the proposed method for compressor health monitoring has been validated using actual operational data from an F-class gas turbine.

title: Acoustic Signal-Based Smart Attendance System for Preventing Fraudulent Attendees
authors: Yeonwoo Sea, Jihwan Park, Yunji Ha, Sihyung Lee, Jiyeon Lee
abstract: Student attendance verification plays a critical role in educational evaluation, yet current smart attendance systems relying on one-time authentication codes are vulnerable to fraudulent attendance due to insider code sharing. This study proposes a novel acoustic signal-based smart attendance system that enhances security without sacrificing usability or requiring additional hardware. The system employs a majority-based authentication mechanism, validating attendance only if more than half of a continuous acoustic signal is received, thereby preventing unauthorized access even when codes are leaked or shared. To address challenges such as asynchronous signal reception and brute-force attacks, a pattern-matching algorithm and probabilistic security analysis are introduced. Experimental results demonstrate 100% authentication accuracy under typical classroom noise within an average of 13 seconds, and robust defense against signal leakage attacks including recorded audio and real-time phone transmission. The proposed system offers a practical, secure, and device-agnostic solution for reliable attendance verification in educational settings.

title: Joint Optimization of Routing and Scheduling in Cross-Domain Deterministic Networks
authors: Xiaolong Wang, Haipeng Yao, Lin Zhu, Wenji He, Wei Zhang, Mohsen Guizani
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Industrial Internet applications require networks to guarantee deterministic end-to-end latency and zero packet loss at both the data link and network layers. Traditional best-effort communication models in consumer networks are insufficient to meet these stringent demands. To meet these stringent demands, the IEEE 802.1 standards introduce Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) at the data link layer, while the IETF proposes Deterministic Networking (DetNet) for the network layer. However, enabling seamless cross-domain communication between TSN and DetNet remains a significant challenge. This paper proposes a unified cross-domain network architecture and a time-slot alignment strategy that compensates for synchronization errors between the TSN and DetNet layers. We further develop a Joint Routing and Scheduling algorithm for Deterministic Cross-Domain Transmission (JRS-DCT), which simultaneously addresses routing and scheduling under cross-domain constraints. The algorithm leverages Cycle-Specified Queuing and Forwarding (CSQF) in DetNet and Cycle Queuing and Forwarding (CQF) in TSN to ensure bounded latency and deterministic transmission. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed JRS-DCT algorithm significantly improves the scheduling success rate and effectively reduces network resource utilization compared to two baseline algorithms. These results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework in supporting time-sensitive communication across heterogeneous network environments.

title: An 8-bit 4.8-GS/s Four-Channel Time-Interleaved SAR ADC With Ping-Pong Comparators Using Global-Dither-Based Timing-Skew Calibration and Bit-Distribution-Based Offset Calibration
authors: Yunsong Tao, Mingyang Gu, Yi Zhong, Lu Jie, Nan Sun
abstract: This article presents a global-dither-based timing-skew calibration technique for time-interleaved (TI) analog-to-digital converters (ADCs). By injecting a dithered reference signal globally at the input buffer, the proposed method ensures negligible residual timing skews after calibration. A dither-injection scheme based on source-follower (SF) addition is proposed to suppress the dither kickback and enable a single-tap dither-removal mechanism. In addition, a bit-distribution-based offset calibration method is introduced to support high-speed successive-approximation-register (SAR) ADCs employing ping-pong comparators without an additional calibration phase. Fabricated in a 28-nm CMOS process, a prototype 8-bit 4.8-GS/s four-channel TI SAR ADC achieves 44.3-dB SNDR and 58.2-dB SFDR at Nyquist. The ADC consumes 10.4 mW and achieves a Walden figure of merit (FoM) of 16.2 fJ/conv-step, including the input buffer and the on-chip calibration engine, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed background calibration techniques for high-speed TI SAR ADCs.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Pareto Optimization: Trade-off Characterization for Assist-as-Needed Training and Performance Evaluation
authors: Harun Tolasa, Volkan Patoglu
abstract: During human motor skill training and physical rehabilitation, there exists an inherent trade-off between task difficulty and user performance. Characterizing this trade-off is crucial for evaluating user performance, designing assist-as needed (AAN) protocols, and assessing the efficacy of training protocols. In this study, we propose a novel human-in-the loop (HiL) Pareto optimization approach to characterize the trade off between the performance and the perceived challenge level of motor learning or rehabilitation tasks. We adapt Bayesian multi criteria optimization to systematically and efficiently perform HiL Pareto characterizations. Our HiL optimization employs a hybrid model measuring performance with a quantitative metric, while the perceived challenge level is captured with a qualitative metric derived from preference-based user feedback. We demonstrate the utility of the framework through three use cases in the context of a manual skill training task with haptic feedback. First, we demonstrate how the characterized trade-off can be used to design a sample AAN training protocol and evaluate the group level efficacy of the proposed AAN protocol relative to a baseline assistance protocol. Second, we demonstrate that individual-level comparisons of the trade-offs characterized before and after the training session enable fair evaluation of training progress under different assistance levels, providing insights even when users cannot perform the task without assistance. Third, we show that the characterized trade-offs also enable fair performance comparisons across users, since they capture each user's best possible performance at all feasible assistance levels.
