title: Training-Free Ultra Small Model for Universal Sparse Reconstruction in Compressed Sensing
authors: Chaoqing Tang, Huanze Zhuang, Guiyun Tian, Zhenli Zeng, Yi Ding, Wenzhong Liu, Lin Lin, Xiang Bai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Despite large models drive unprecedented growth in data and model parameters, many real-world problems prioritize interpretability and generality, and lack sufficient training data. For instance, in Compressed Sensing (CS) where sparse reconstruction solves underdetermined systems, traditional iterative methods remain the practical choice due to their interpretability and out-of-the-box applicability to arbitrary conditions, but suffer from poor quality and inefficiency at low sampling rates. To address this, we propose Coefficients Learning (CL), a novel training-free framework for sparse reconstruction. CL employs ultra-small neural models with only <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n$</tex-math></inline-formula> trainable parameters for a length-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n$</tex-math></inline-formula> signal. It retains the interpretability and generality of traditional iterative methods by adopting their residual-based solving process, while enhancing efficiency and accuracy by replacing closed-form solutions with automatic differentiation and embedding prior knowledge into the model losses. We evaluate CL extensively on synthetic and real one-dimensional and two-dimensional signals. A detailed analysis is first conducted using an implemented CLOMP. To demonstrate general applicability, CL is also implemented on three types of classic iterative CS reconstruction methods. Results show that CL maintains the generality of iterative methods while significantly boosting accuracy. Although it adds minor overhead for convex optimization or message-passing methods, it achieves efficiency gains of 100 to 1000 times for greedy algorithms. On the tested nine diverse image datasets, CL improves median reconstruction accuracy by approximately 163%, 78%, and 35% at sampling rates of 0.04, 0.25, and 0.5, respectively, compared to classic iterative methods. This training-free CS reconstruction method can truly empower countless industrial or medical machines that rely on sparse solution.

title: Tactile-Guided Exploration and Positioning for High-Precision Robotic Peg-in-Hole Tasks
authors: Yuzhe Huang, Wanlin Li, Ziyuan Jiao
abstract: Robotic peg-in-hole tasks are fundamental to many industrial applications but remain challenging due to the need for precise component alignment. Vision-based systems enable effective object detection, but their pose estimation accuracy often degrades in the presence of hardware noise and environmental disturbances. To address this, we present <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Tac</u>tile-guided <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">E</u>xploration and <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">P</u>ositioning (TacEP), a framework that integrates visual and tactile sensing for high-precision positioning of the target receptacle in robotic peg-in-hole tasks. TacEP first estimates the receptacle’s pose from a monocular camera. To compensate for errors during camera estimation, the system reactively adjusts the pose of a high-resolution Vision-Based Tactile Sensor through real-time exploration, enabling finer positioning of the receptacle. We evaluated TacEP on a physical robotic system across peg-in-hole tasks with five daily connectors, achieving an 81.0% success rate with mean position and orientation errors of 0.37 mm and 0.48<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula>, respectively. Compared to the vision-only baseline, TacEP achieved a 97.7% reduction in position error and an 89.5% reduction in orientation error. These results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly improves insertion reliability and accuracy, highlighting the potential of tactile sensing for high-precision robotic peg-in-hole tasks.

title: Thermal Modeling and Analysis of Cryogenic Nitrogen Flow Boiling through Micro-Pin Fin Channels
authors: Mohammad Bintang Fikri, Sohyeon Joo, Kyoung Joon Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: This study has investigated the cryogenic nitrogen flow boiling in inline micro-pin fin channels (MPFCs) using computational fluid dynamics (CFD) modeling and a Bayesian neural network (BNN). The CFD model, employing a coupled level-set and volume of fluid (CLSVOF) approach, has tracked the flow boiling process of nitrogen within the MPFC. The CFD model has been validated against established correlations and prior experimental data. The investigation has explored the effects of fin spacing and applied heat flux on the heat transfer coefficient. Results have shown that CFD-calculated heat transfer coefficients range from 32.3 to 82.2 kW/m²-K at a mass flow rate of 2.5 g/s, aligning with experimental findings with a typical discrepancy of 25%. A comparison with the Bertsch correlation has revealed a 20% average discrepancy mainly due to the dissimilar geometry to plain micro-channels. This discrepancy might be explained by the micro-pin fins enabling the increase of the convective mixing and heat transfer enhancement. The BNN has predicted slightly higher, yet consistent, heat transfer coefficient values ranging from 42.9 to 93.6 kW/m²-K, showing similar trends to the CFD results. The study has also found that at higher heat fluxes such as 700 and 900 kW/m², the heat transfer coefficient has increased asymptotically with fin spacing. This trend has not been observed at a lower heat flux such as 300 kW/m². A coupled effect of heat flux, fin spacing, and vapor dynamics could explain this interesting behavior.

title: DSP-Based Capacitor Voltage Estimation for Flying Capacitor Multilevel Inverters
authors: Biyang Yu, Hua Han, Yao Sun, Shiming Xie, Jiong Li, Lifang Ha
abstract: To achieve a fast dynamic response in a flying capacitor multilevel inverter (FCMLI), accurate capacitor voltage information is essential. However, as the number of levels increases, directly measuring capacitor voltages requires additional sampling channels and sensors, which significantly raises the cost. To address this issue, this article proposes a capacitor voltage estimation method based on a parameter estimation-based observer (PEBO). To implement this observer on a digital controller, discretization is necessary. However, the presence of nonsmooth switching functions poses significant challenges to the observer’s discretization. Traditional fixed-step discretization methods require very small step sizes to achieve accurate voltage estimation. This requirement leads to a heavy computational burden and necessitates the use of high-speed digital controllers, such as FPGAs. To overcome this limitation, we propose an efficient discretization method based on exponential integration with variable steps. This method reduces the computational burden while maintaining accurate capacitor voltage estimation, which makes the observer feasible to implement in widely used DSPs. Finally, the experimental results verify the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: EasyVoid: A Handheld Coandă Effect Uroflowmeter for Accurate and Portable Measurement of Urinary Flow Dynamics
authors: Ray Chang, Yu-Chen Chen, Hao-Wei Chen, Ruei-Hong Zeng, Manu Prakash
publish: IEEE Journal of Translational Engineering in Health and Medicine
abstract: Objective: Diagnosis of lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) often relies on subjective questionnaires, conventional uroflowmetry or invasive urodynamic study, each with inherent limitations. Methods and procedures: This work presents EasyVoid, a novel handheld device that employs the Coandă effect to enable non-invasive and quantitative measurement of urinary flow. EasyVoid uses a passive fluidic mechanism in which the urinary stream adheres to a curved surface and transfers angular momentum to a helical rotor, allowing direct estimation of flow rate and volume without pressure sensors or weighing systems. Results: EasyVoid simultaneously records key voiding parameters, including flow rate, flow pattern, voiding time, and voided volume, with high temporal precision. In controlled bench-top trials using simulated urine events, measurements obtained from EasyVoid showed strong agreement with standard uroflowmetry across multiple flow patterns. Conclusion: These results demonstrate the potential of EasyVoid for portable and quantitative assessment of urinary flow dynamics, offering a promising solution for remote urinary health monitoring. Clinical and Translational Impact Statement: EasyVoid demonstrates the potential for portable, quantitative assessment of urinary flow to support future home-based care and longitudinal monitoring of lower urinary tract symptoms. Category: Early/Pre-Clinical Research.

title: Deep Spectral Feature Learning With Evolutionary Band Selection for Environmental Monitoring of Plastic Debris
authors: R. Anand
abstract: The proliferation of plastic debris in terrestrial and aquatic environments poses significant ecological and monitoring challenges worldwide. Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) provides fine-grained spectral signatures that enable the reliable identification of plastic types; however, its high dimensionality introduces redundancy, computational burden, and susceptibility to the Hughes phenomenon. To address these issues, this study proposes a two-phase framework that integrates Superb Fairy-Wren Optimization (SFWO) for band selection with a band-specific pooling spectral Convolutional Neural Network (BSPS-CNN) for classification. In the first phase, the SFWO operates as a wrapper-based metaheuristic that selects an informative and non-redundant band subset by optimizing a fitness function that combines the signal-to-noise ratio, variance, and inter-band correlation. In the second phase, the BSPS-CNN employs multi-scale one-dimensional convolutions, concatenation, and band-specific global average pooling to extract discriminative spectral features from the reduced hyperspectral cube. The resulting framework improves the plastic debris classification accuracy while substantially reducing the input dimensionality and computational cost. Experiments on hyperspectral plastic debris datasets demonstrated that the proposed approach outperformed existing band selection and deep learning methods in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and robustness.

title: Analysis of acoustic vibrational modes in Germanium quantum dot in Silicon systems for light-harvesting applications
authors: Natdanai Khongpetch, Pawaphat Jaturaphagorn, Nisachon Tangsangiumvisai, Papichaya Chaisakul
abstract: This article proposes the utilization of acoustic signals through vibrational modes of Germanium (Ge) quantum dots (QDs) in order to potentially detect changes in the QD’s physical characteristics. Above all, we find that the proposed vibrational mode analysis could enable in-situ measurement of QD devices during their manufacturing process, which would not be possible using traditional time-consuming area-specific techniques such as expensive electron microscope devices. This thereby ensures the desired characteristics of the QD device well before finishing the fabrication. Based upon the Finite-Different Time-Domain method and the Finite Element Method, extensive simulation results demonstrate empirically that the analysis of acoustic signals detected from the Ge QDs can be employed for physical variation detection, including size and density. Significantly, we show that the proposed method is potentially applicable in detecting critical physical variations of QDs effecting the light harvesting characteristic of the Ge QDs embedded in a Si-based p-i-n diode structure.

title: Decoupled Measurement Method of Windage and Bearing Losses in High-Speed Electric Machines for Heavy-Duty Automotive Applications
authors: Haibo Long, Tenghui Dong, Chong Zhu, Xiaodong Wu, Xi Zhang, Yong Zhang, Ronggao Cui
abstract: With the rapid development of transportation electrification, the elevated speed range of automotive traction motors brings a significant increase in the windage and the bearing losses (collectively termed mechanical losses), posing additional considerations for motor design. However, windage and bearing losses are influenced by various non-intuitive factors of the rotation-support system, including the rotor stiffness, the metal surface, etc. Therefore, measurements are necessary in addition to numerical simulation or theoretical estimation to achieve a reliable evaluation. However, bearing loss tests will generate substantial windage losses simultaneously during the dragging test. So, the measurement methods must decouple the two loss types for accurate quantification. This paper proposes a novel measurement method of windage and bearing losses following the principle of equivalence and differentiation. The automotive traction motors' windage and bearing losses in a wide speed range (0~15,000 rpm) are measured and analyzed. This research provides an essential reference for subsequent motor efficiency optimization and thermal prediction, especially for large-sized traction motors in heavy-duty vehicles.

title: Mixed Reality Golf Putting: a Comparative Analysis of Golf Putting Performance in Real, Virtual, and Augmented Virtuality Environments_supp1-3679107.pdf
authors: Ke Li
abstract: Although immersive technologies hold enormous potential for enhancing sports training, existing systems encounter notable limitations. While fully immersive virtual reality (VR) systems can address constraints of physical training setups such as limited space or equipment, full VR environments often lack rich, realistic, and multisensory feedback often required for effective sports training. To address this gap, this work investigates skill-based sport training, particularly, golf putting, across the reality–virtuality continuum. We implemented a mixed reality (MR) putting simulator that combines real equipment (putter and golf ball) with a virtual training environment. Three training conditions were realized: (i) real-world putting, constrained by limited physical space; (ii) a VR putting, in which a real putter and ball are used but represented virtually within the virtual environment (VE); (iii) an augmented virtuality (AV) putting, which reveals the real putter, ball, and users' limbs through a localized video-passthrough window, aiming to preserve users' sense of embodiment and presence while enabling simulation of an unlimited putting area. We conducted a pilot within-subjects experiment with 36 participants and found that the AV condition improved immediate putting performance, reduced task load, reduced perceived motion sickness, and increased user preference compared to the VR condition, while user's sense of presence remained comparable to VR training. These findings indicate that AV offers a pragmatic “sweet spot” on the virtuality continuum, balancing the trade-off between sensory fidelity and training flexibility, offering valuable design insights for immersive sports training systems.

title: Arbitrary-Scale Point Cloud Upsampling with Saliency-Aware Implicit Surface Guidance
authors: Yanzhe Liu, Rong Chen, Yushi Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Despite significant progress in point cloud upsampling, most existing methods rely heavily on supervised training with paired data, which are often difficult to acquire. Moreover, the inherent lack of explicit connectivity in point clouds makes it difficult to achieve both continuous and uniform densification while accurately recovering fine geometric structures. To address these problems, we propose an upsampling model that treats this task as saliency-aware implicit surface sampling, enabling self-supervised and fine-grained point densification. Central to our idea is correlating implicit surface reconstruction with salient point identification, and carrying out sampling on the saliency-aware surface representation. Motivated by this, we introduce a salient point detector along with a corresponding implicit surface-based interpolator, and a geometry filter, upon which we develop a three-stage architecture consisting of a pre-trained saliency guidance block, a saliency-aware enhancer, and an upsampler. The guidance block captures meaningful shape patterns to prevent detail loss and incomplete recovery, while the enhancer facilitates detail enhancement in complex salient regions. These components are integrated with the upsampler to generate dense results that accurately retain both global shape and meticulous structures. In comparison with state-of-the-art methods, our model significantly improves the upsampling quality. Extensive experiments conducted on various datasets, comprising both synthetic and real-world captured shapes, demonstrate the flexibility and availability of our method in processing the point clouds across different scales and distributions.

title: Diffusion-Based Dynamic Contract for Federated AI Agent Construction in Mobile Metaverses_supp1-3665640.pdf
authors: Zhang Yang
abstract: Mobile metaverses are envisioned as a transformative digital ecosystem that delivers immersive, intelligent, and ubiquitous services through mobile devices. Driven by Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs), Artificial Intelligence (AI) agents hold the potential to empower the creation, maintenance, and evolution of mobile metaverses, enabling seamless human-machine interaction and dynamic service adaptation. Currently, AI agents are primarily built upon cloud-based LLMs and VLMs. However, several challenges hinder their efficient deployment, including high service latency and a risk of sensitive data leakage during perception and processing. In this paper, we develop an edge-cloud collaboration-based federated AI agent construction framework in mobile metaverses. Specifically, Edge Servers (ESs), as agent infrastructures, first create agent modules in a distributed manner. The cloud server then integrates these modules into AI agents and deploys them at the edge, thereby enabling low-latency AI agent services for users. Considering that ESs may exhibit dynamic levels of willingness to participate in federated AI agent construction, we design a two-period dynamic contract model to continuously incentivize ESs to participate in agent module creation, effectively addressing the dynamic information asymmetry between the cloud server and ESs. Furthermore, we propose an Enhanced Diffusion Model-based Soft Actor-Critic (EDMSAC) algorithm to effectively generate optimal dynamic contracts. In the algorithm, we apply dynamic structured pruning to DM-based actor networks to enhance denoising efficiency and policy learning performance. Simulation results demonstrate that the EDMSAC algorithm outperforms the DMSAC algorithm by up to 23% in optimal dynamic contract generation.

title: Ultra High Definition Video Quality Assessment Based on Adaptively Spatial Fusion and Temporal Selection
authors: Zhijie Liang, Wei Sheng, Haoran Ma, Fei Zhou, Guoping Qiu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Ultra high definition video quality assessment (UHD VQA) is challenging due to the high spatio-temporal dimensions of UHD content. In this work, we propose a spatio-temporal framework based on feature fusion and selection to facilitate UHD VOA. First, we introduce a spatially adaptive fusion net (SAFNet) that integrates all patch-wise features into a compact frame-wise representation via local aggregation and global interaction. During the aggregation and the interaction, the contribution of each patch-wise feature is initially estimated via visual saliency-derived weights and then adaptively refined by contextual adjustments. Second, we design a temporally adaptive selection net (TASNet) to reduces temporal redundancy. It retains a small number of temporal positions through thresholding a dynamically estimated importance vector, which captures both representativeness and inter-frame variation. Finally, extensive experiments conducted on 4 datasets with various 4K videos demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in comparison with some traditional and state-of-the-art VQA methods.

title: Structural Time-Admissibility as a Safety Interlock in Networked Systems
authors: Hyunkeun Shin
abstract: Legacy message-centric communication standards define success by delivery completion. In time-critical operations, this delivery-centric definition allows information to arrive after its operational validity has expired, consuming resources and influencing decisions despite being obsolete. This paper identifies this condition as Structural Time Failure (STF), a structural mismatch between communication success and time-admissible operational validity. To address STF without modifying existing standards, we introduce the Reaction Window (RW) as a human-defined temporal authority and propose a pre-transmission time-admissible overlay architecture that excludes time-invalid messages before they enter the delivery process. Unlike conventional delay or congestion mitigation techniques, the proposed mechanism enforces temporal admissibility as a structural condition independent of network capacity. Discrete-event simulation results demonstrate that delivery-centric systems inevitably experience STF under load, whereas the proposed architecture preserves time-valid delivery for critical traffic by preventing obsolete information from entering transmission, queueing, and actuation paths. This paper does not propose a new protocol nor optimize network performance, but analyzes and enforces temporal admissibility as a structural safety condition. This work establishes a conceptual architecture for temporal admissibility enforcement as a structural safety interlock, rather than a protocol optimization.

title: Adaptive and Lightweight DDoS Defense in SDN-Enabled Edge–IoT Networks Using Unsupervised and Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ananth Kumar R, Ramachandra Reddy, Bharath Kumar S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The high rate of growth in the Internet of Things (IoT) devices within the edge computing settings has grown the vulnerability of the networks to Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks exponentially and necessitates efficient, adaptive, and defensive solutions. In this work, a novel SDN-Edge-IoT-DDoS detection and mitigation system is suggested which makes use of an unsupervised-powered anomaly detection model along with a reinforcement learning-based countermeasures in mitigation strategies. In contrast to the traditional methods based on a fixed threshold or signature-like detection, the suggested solution constantly hears traffic patterns at the Software-Defined Networking (SDN) controller and changes mitigation policies dynamically with the current network behavior and predicts potential attack vectors before they cause a substantial disruption. The framework uses a lightweight feature extraction that is adapted to resourceconstrained IoT devices and thus it has minimal overhead. The results of simulation prove that the methodology positively influences detection accuracy, false positives and resource amount usage, creating a self-learning, context-aware, and predictive security paradigm of SDN-enabled edge-IoT networks. This represents a novel integration of predictive Machine learning (ML)-based mitigation being integrated into an SDN-edge-IoT ecosystem.

title: A Fast Decoding Algorithm for Generalized Reed-Solomon Codes and Alternant Codes
authors: Nianqi Tang, Yunghsiang S. Han, Danyang Pei, Chao Chen
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: In this paper, it is shown that the syndromes of generalized Reed-Solomon (GRS) codes and alternant codes can be characterized in terms of inverse fast Fourier transform. Then, a fast decoding algorithm is proposed, which has a computational complexity of O(n log (n-k)+(n-k) log<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>(n-k)) for all (n, k) GRS codes and (n, k) alternant codes. Particularly, this provides a new decoding method for Goppa codes, which is an important subclass of alternant codes. When decoding the binary Goppa code with a length of 8192 and a correction capability of 128, the new algorithm is nearly 10 times faster than traditional methods. The decoding algorithm is suitable for the McEliece cryptosystem, which is a candidate for post-quantum cryptography techniques.

title: Automatic Rain-Sensing Wiper
authors: Akshat Jain, Hemalatha S, G Deepa
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Today, in the automotive industry, the important factor is to ensure the safety of people travelling in cars. For a long time, car wipers work on a manual switching. Recent incidents have shown that faulty wipers and improper manual speed adjustments have contributed to accidents, their speed not being adjusted manually have caused accidents. In this paper, we propose an automatic rain sensing wiper system which starts operating when the rain starts and stops when there is no rain. The automatic wiper controller is intended to assist and provide more comfort to the drivers by minimizing distractions. This wiper system in a car detects rainfall and gets activated automatically. When the sensor detects raindrops, it senses the intensity of rain and changes the wiper speed accordingly so that the required rotation rate is achieved. This eliminates the requirement of car wipers needing to be controlled manually. The design of automated rain wiper involves the following components: an Arduino Uno, 2A Digital Rain Drop Sensors Module with Converter, Servo Motor and an External Power Supply. Rain drop sensor detects the rain and transfers the command to the Servomotor which is then processed by the Arduino. The rain drop sensor is installed outside the car and on the side of the windshield and connected to the servo motor which is connected to the Wiper Blade. The proposed wiper system is cost effective and is designed to ensure energy efficient using Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) to control the servo motor speed. This wiper system controls the movement of wipers with a response time below 400 ms.

title: A Novel Optimizer Based Deep Neural Network Model for IoMT
authors: Buditi Sriveni, Naveen Kumar Navuri, Korra Srinivas, Tirumala Paruchuri, Jaganathan Muthusamy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Intelligent diagnostics and real-time monitoring in the field of Internet of Things (IoMT) is changing modern healthcare, which also raises the risk of cyber threats, which makes important to get a strong intrusion detection systems (IDS). The current research used the MSCAD dataset to show the efficiency of eight popular optimizer for training the deep neural network model, by considering the real IoMT network conditions. Analyzing different performance metrices shows that the DNN optimized with Adam performs the best as compared to others, achieving a precision of 0.97753. This level of accuracy shows just how important it is to choose the right optimizer when building effective deep learning-based intrusion detection systems for medical networks. The proposed DNN-Adam method provides useful information for creating secure and successful cybersecurity solutions for limited resources, real-time healthcare systems by enhancing detection accuracy, reliability and flexibility in the IoMT environment.

title: Spatiotemporal Forecasting of Renewable Energy Using Hybrid Networks in Tamil Nadu
authors: Jinu Sophia J, Harshni P, Helina Sergius D
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Tamil Nadu's prime source of generating renewable energy primarily derives from sustainable solar and wind resources, but the presence of climate-related variability and extreme weather events still disrupts traditional methods of power generation and significantly complicates the execution of gridrelated duties for the regulating body. In this research, a unique hybridized deep-learning architecture, utilising both ConvLSTM and a Transformer that relies on Time-Series, is proposed as an innovative solution to this challenge and to provide greater accuracy for spatiotemporal power forecast modelling. ConvLSTM effectively models both “short-term” and “spatial” characteristics, as demonstrated by recent studies concerning the accurate forecasting of electricity flow and loads, respectively, at several geographically separated sites. Current academic literature on this subject reinforces the need for a predictive technology that has capabilities to be utilized in a variety of geographical regions as well as offshore applications and services regarding wind generation infrastructure. By synthesizing the advantages of both architectures, this unique hybridized mode design is expected to produce consistent and reliable results that can be employed to improve smart grid management and provide support for decision making. Ultimately, we hope this model will support Tamil Nadu's energy resource development efforts.

title: A Meta-Model Based Framework for Dynamic Entity Management in Big Data Systems
authors: Roopam Dhanraj Choudhari, Gaurav Khodwe
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The proliferation of heterogeneous data types in modern big data systems presents significant challenges for traditional data management approaches. This paper proposes a meta-model-based framework that enables dynamic entity classification, validation, and management without requiring custom implementations for each data type. The framework addresses key big data challenges including schema evolution, data variety, and scalable validation. Present the architectural design, implementation considerations, and theoretical analysis demonstrating potential for significant improvements in development efficiency and system flexibility. Through architectural analysis and comparison with traditional approaches, show that the framework can theoretically reduce development overhead by up to 80% while enabling rapid deployment of new entity types. The framework's applicability is demonstrated through detailed design analysis and projected performance characteristics based on established metamodeling principles.

title: Improved Channel Estimation Scheme for STAR-RIS-Assisted Communication Systems
authors: Shibsankar Das, Adrish Banerjee
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surface (STAR-RIS) is a new paradigm in which the impinging signals are divided into transmitted and reflected signals to facilitate full-space network coverage. In this paper, we study a novel extended least-square (ELS) channel estimation design for a STAR-RIS-aided uplink communication system. We propose an ELS scheme to efficiently estimate the channel vectors under the time-switching (TS) and energysplitting (ES) protocols for the STAR-RIS-assisted system. We show that the mean square error (MSE) of the proposed ELS channel estimation scheme is always lower than the MSE of the existing LS scheme. The effectiveness of our proposed channel estimation approach is validated through numerical results and theoretical analysis.

title: Identification of Cyber-Physical Security Boundary for Rotating Bearings under Attacks Using PINNs
authors: Ziyue Jia, Yiying Tao, Hanqi Zhou, Siqi Meng, Yuheng Li, Qingao Yuan, Yang Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Malicious attacks pose a significant threat to the operational safety of rotating machinery by inducing speed fluctuations that modify load conditions, intensify stress, and accelerate fatigue damage. To bridge the gap between cyber-layer disturbances and their physical consequences, this paper proposes a FEA–PPgNN framework that shifts protection from the cyber domain to the physical domain. By coupling speed fluctuations with stress and fatigue behavior, a cyber–physical security boundary is defined to represent the bearing’s intrinsic safe operating limit and enable physical-domain defense against cyber attacks. A transient finite-element analysis (FEA) model is developed to generate speed–stress datasets, followed by a data-driven neural network that predicts equivalent stress under arbitrary speeds. The stress outputs are subsequently fed into a physics-informed neural network (PINN) to estimate fatigue life under embedded physical constraints. The cyber–physical security boundary is quantitatively derived through the proposed FEA–PPgNN framework, which integrates stress prediction and fatigue evaluation to identify the physical threshold where mechanical vulnerability emerges and the transition from safe operation to cyber-induced failure occurs. Computational experiments are conducted on a generator rotating bearing, and the results demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively identify the mechanical safety boundary under cyber-induced disturbances.

title: A Lightweight Approach to Multipath Traffic Splitting Against Website Fingerprinting
authors: Zirui Zhang, Jilong Yang, Weichen Li, Jincheng Deng, Chuanrong Liu, Lina Wang, Jin Zhao
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Website Fingerprinting (WFP) attacks pose a significant threat to user privacy in anonymous communication networks such as Tor by exploiting encrypted traffic metadata to infer users’ browsing behavior. State-of-the-art deep learning-based classifiers can achieve high accuracy when targeting undefended traffic. Existing defense mechanisms face a fundamental trade-off: while traffic padding and shaping provide strong protection, they incur substantial bandwidth and latency overhead; in contrast, multipath-based approaches enhance privacy by distributing traffic across multiple paths, though often at the cost of increased complexity.We present LW-MTS, a lightweight multipath traffic splitting defense that processes only a configurable fraction of packets via sampling-based splitting. Our simulation-based evaluation on standard datasets and attacks shows that LW-MTS reduces attack accuracy under our primary concatenate scenario, provides strong protection in single-path settings, and maintains residual protection against adaptive attackers. The defense requires only a small number of concurrent circuits and adds low latency derived from empirical circuit latencies, offering a practical operating point that balances privacy protection with operational efficiency.

title: Digital Correction of Spatial–Spectral Aberrations in a Mobile Hyperspectral System
authors: Daniil Iu. Gonoshilkin, Margarita S. Shatkovskaya, Nikita M. Sokolov, Dmitry R. Sharivzyanov
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: This paper describes a calibration workflow for a mobile LED-based hyperspectral scanning system operating in the 400–1000 nm range. We show that an iterative strategy alternating geometric correction and lateral chromatic aberration alignment yields the lowest residual distortion and stable interband registration. A reproducible approach based on polynomial transforms and a printed grid target is proposed for integration into mobile devices.

title: Blockchain-Based Secure and Composable Model for Semi-Honest Privacy-Preserving Computation
authors: Longyang Yi, Jian Liu, Zhiguo Wan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Privacy-preserving computation enables multiple parties to jointly compute a function while keeping their inputs private. Protocols designed for the semi-honest model achieve high efficiency by assuming participants will correctly follow the protocol’s cryptographic steps. However, this security assumption is confined to the protocol’s internal execution, creating a crucial accountability gap. It offers no inherent method to prove that the inputs and function used in the computation actually align with what was externally agreed upon. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that enhances privacy-preserving computation with public verifiability and accountability, while maintaining composability. Our framework leverages a blockchain as an immutable trust anchor to register cryptographic commitments of both participant inputs and the function’s specification. We then employ a zero-knowledge proof protocol to verify that the privacy-preserving computation is performed correctly using the committed data and function logic. The security of our model is formally proven to guarantee both input privacy and computational integrity, while our performance evaluation shows its practical scalability.

title: Blockchain-Based Distributed Identity Supervision and Revocation
authors: Qiang Gao, Zhiqi Liu, Youqi Li, Cong Zuo, Licheng Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Decentralized Identity (DID) systems have gained wide attention due to their advantages in autonomy, interoperability, and openness. However, existing solutions still have significant shortcomings in the adaptability of malicious detection, the granularity of identity revocation, and process collaboration. This paper proposes an autonomous identity supervision and revocation scheme based on a rewritable blockchain: by introducing the XGBoost model and extracting on-chain multi-dimensional features, high detection accuracy is maintained even in extreme scenarios; meanwhile, three types of fine-grained scenarios are designed, covering voluntary deactivation by users and system-triggered malicious revocation; efficient transaction updates are achieved via chameleon hash, and compliance is ensured by combining the PBFT consensus mechanism; finally, a full-process collaborative system of "detection-revocation-evidence storage" is constructed. Experimental results show that the scheme maintains low latency in DID registration, identity verification, and identity revocation processes, operates stably, and effectively addresses the technical gaps in existing solutions.

title: Optimizing Industrial Automation: A Multi-Algorithm Machine Learning Approach for Predicting Robotics Adoption Impact
authors: D. Sungeetha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Implementation of industrial robotics is a decision-making process that is very critical to manufacturing organizations that should thoroughly evaluate various interdependent variables such as productivity, cost savings, workforce displacement and training needs. Hence a 14 different machine learning models on a variety of performance indices and the best model was LightGBM that demonstrated almost perfect training performance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(R^{2}=0.0001)$</tex> and cross-validation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(R^{2}=-0.1797)$</tex>. The model includes in-depth feature analysis via correlation studies and feature rankings, which show that robots adopted, cost saving, and job displaced are the three factors that most significantly contribute to productivity savings. This approach will utilize a comparative analysis of algorithms based on classical regression methods to the latest ensemble techniques and neural networks through systematic comparison in terms of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> scores, RMSE, and MAE. The comparisons of the residual analysis and the prediction and the actual values indicate that the model is reliable enough to be deployed in the real world. The framework will offer manufacturing decision-makers an information-based planning instrument on strategic robotics investments decision-making based on technological progress and the workforce and financial optimization. The study adds significance in the domain as it has developed a repeatable methodology of multi-objective optimization of industrial automation

title: Soft-DDQN-Based Frequency Selection Strategy in Time-Slotted Channel Hopping Networks
authors: Siming Pang, Xianhua Niu, Shiyang Zhou, Bing Liu, Bosen Zeng, Pengxu Chen
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Time-Slotted Channel Hopping (TSCH) in IEEE 802.15.4e improves anti-jamming via precise time-frequency scheduling, but most reinforcement learning (RL)-based methods only consider Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR), neglecting switching delays and thus reducing performance. This paper proposes a Soft-DDQN-based frequency selection strategy for TSCH networks, consisting of a framework and an algorithm. The framework models channel dynamics with a PDR-matrix-based environment and designs a hybrid reward function combining instantaneous quality, trend incentives, and hopping penalties. The algorithm applies Soft Double DQN with soft target updates and double Q -value estimation, enabling channel selection per time slot to avoid low-quality links, trained via experience replay and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\epsilon$</tex>-greedy exploration. These enhancements mitigate Q-value overestimation, reduce policy oscillation, and improve training stability by 43.08% over Double-DQN.

title: Analysis and Application of a Discrete-Time Neurodynamic Approach for Fast Constrained $l_{1}$-Norm Minimization
authors: Youshen Xia, Jun Wang, Changyin Sun
abstract: Discrete-time neurodynamic approaches (also called recurrent neural networks (RNNs)) are easily implemented on software and simulated on digital circuits. First, this paper proposes two modified discrete-time RNNs for quickly dealing with constrained <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$l_{1}$</tex>-norm minimization problems. Next, the two modified discrete-time RNNs are proven to be globally convergent to an optimal solution under a large step size. Finally, we apply the obtained results for image recovery. Two convergent discrete-time RNN based algorithms for non-blind image restoration are presented. Due to having a low complexity, the two discrete-time RNNs are more computationally efficient than the existing discrete-time RNN for image restoration. Computed results with application examples show that the two discrete-time RNN-based algorithms are indeed superior to the existing discrete-time RNN-based algorithms with regards to computation time.

authors: N/A

title: Quantum Artificial Intelligence: From Quantum Neural Networks to Self-Programming Architectures [Feature]
authors: Samuel Yen-Chi Chen
abstract: Quantum Artificial Intelligence (QAI) represents the convergence of quantum computing and machine learning, aiming to harness the computational power of quantum mechanics to enhance the efficiency, scalability, and generalization capability of intelligent systems. This article provides a tutorial on the foundations, models, and emerging directions of QAI. We first review the core concepts of quantum information and hybrid quantum–classical computing, followed by a detailed discussion of variational quantum circuits (VQCs)—the fundamental building blocks of quantum neural networks (QNNs). Building upon these foundations, we introduce representative QAI architectures, including Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNNs) for pattern recognition, Quantum Long Short-Term Memory (QLSTM) for sequential modeling, and Quantum Reinforcement Learning (QRL) for decision making and control. Advanced topics such as Quantum Architecture Search (QAS), Quantum Fast Weight Programmers (QFWP), and the Quantum-Train (QT) framework are presented to illustrate how classical and quantum models can dynamically co-design and reprogram one another. Furthermore, we discuss federated and distributed quantum learning, encoding complexity, and training challenges such as barren plateaus and hardware constraints. The article concludes with a forward-looking outlook on scalable QAI systems, autonomous quantum agents, and the long-term vision of self-programming, distributed intelligence. By bridging quantum theory, learning algorithms, and system architectures, this work aims to offer both foundational understanding and strategic insights for the next generation of hybrid quantum-classical intelligence.

title: Quantification and Correction of SMAP Soil Moisture Retrieval Biases Induced by Subpixel Moisture and Texture Heterogeneity over China_supp1-3679771.docx
authors: Xiaojun Li
abstract: Surface soil moisture (SSM) plays a vital role in regulating terrestrial water and energy exchanges. However, SSM retrievals from passive microwave (PMW) sensors such as Soil Moisture Active Passive (SMAP) are theoretically biased by subpixel soil heterogeneity, particularly the combined variability of soil moisture and soil texture within coarse-resolution footprints. This study investigates and corrects these heterogeneity-induced biases in SMAP SSM products. Using a 1-km downscaled SSM dataset, we quantify how fine-scale soil moisture variability propagates through the nonlinear radiative transfer model (RTM) and induces systematic dry biases at the coarse scale. Specifically, the bias magnitude increases nonlinearly with the subpixel standard deviation of SSM, an indicator of soil moisture heterogeneity, and is further amplified by soil texture variability. Fitting this relationship with a quadratic function enables the construction of pixel-specific bias correction models. Validation against dense in situ measurements from automatic soil moisture stations in China shows that, in the best-case scenario, the correction reduces ubRMSE from 0.044 to 0.033 cm³/cm³ and improves correlation from 0.80 to 0.90. A nationwide assessment (2016–2023) reveals that relative bias is generally low (1–5%) but can exceed 15% in strongly heterogeneous regions and reach up to 50% under extreme conditions. Overall, the findings underscore the necessity of accounting for subpixel soil moisture and texture heterogeneity to enhance the accuracy of PMW-derived SSM products. The proposed correction approach is transferable to current and forthcoming microwave missions, including Soil Moisture and Ocean Salinity Mission (SMOS) and Copernicus Imaging Microwave Radiometer (CIMR).

title:
KMoPSO
                    <sub>TE</sub>
                    : Advancing Self-Driving Networks with a Knowledge-Driven Multi-Objective Particle Swarm Optimization Algorithm for Traffic Engineering
authors: Hong Zhao, Ling Tang, Li-Guang Xie, Jian-Yu Li, Jing Liu
abstract: As communication networks become increasingly complex and expansive, Internet Service Providers (ISPs) face significant challenges in sustaining network efficiency and responsiveness. Traditional traffic engineering approaches, such as local search and linear programming, often struggle to swiftly adapt to changing network conditions and can be computationally intensive for large-scale networks. Therefore, this paper proposes the Knowledge-driven Multi-objective Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm for Traffic Engineering (KMoPSO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TE</sub>), an innovative solution that addresses the limitations of current approaches by simultaneously considering multiple network performance objectives. The KMOPSO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TE</sub> offers three key benefits. First, it integrates segment routing with equal-cost multi-path (ECMP) into a multi-objective optimization framework for dynamic routing control. Second, it employs an adaptive particle encoding strategy that efficiently determines routing paths aligned with network topologies. Third, a dynamic knowledge-driven update strategy intelligently adjusts link weights based on network attributes, distinguishing it from static optimization methods. These features collectively enhance network performance and adaptability. KMoPSO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TE</sub> is designed to maintain excellent performance in larger network sizes and has undergone comprehensive evaluations using real-world network topologies from the Internet Topology Zoo dataset. The experimental results indicate that when compared with state-of-the-art algorithms, KMoPSO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TE</sub> achieves an average reduction of 30.58% in Maximum Link Utilization, thereby showcasing its enhancements in network performance, adaptability, and generalization capabilities within large-scale network environments.
